Ownership includes direct, beneficial, trust, corporate, partnership, nominee, or controlling fractional interests. No household may use multiple nominal owners to evade the limits. Temporary exceptions may be granted for inheritance, divorce, replacement construction, relocation, casualty, or estate administration, ordinarily for no more than twenty-four months.
Section 2 — Primary Residence

Every adult may designate only one Primary Residence, which must be actually occupied for most of the year except during temporary absence for medical care, education, military service, work assignment, caregiving, disaster, or comparable necessity.

textBlocks.text: Housing shall serve first as a place of residence and human stability rather than an unrestricted vehicle for speculation. This Article establishes limits on residential accumulation, a guaranteed path to first ownership, controlled transfer values, nonprofit rental operation, mandatory income-based affordability, and conversion of unused commercial property when lowest-tier housing demand is unmet.
Section 1 — Residential Ownership Limits

Ownership includes direct, beneficial, trust, corporate, partnership, nominee, or controlling fractional interests. No household may use multiple nominal owners to evade the limits. Temporary exceptions may be granted for inheritance, divorce, replacement construction, relocation, casualty, or estate administration, ordinarily for no more than twenty-four months.
Section 2 — Primary Residence

Every adult may designate only one Primary Residence, which must be actually occupied for most of the year except during temporary absence for medical care, education, military service, work assignment, caregiving, disaster, or comparable necessity.
Section 3 — Prohibition of Corporate Accumulation

For-profit corporations, investment funds, private-equity entities, and similar bodies shall not acquire ordinary homes for passive rent, speculative resale, or appreciation. Permitted institutional ownership includes public housing authorities, nonprofit cooperatives, community land trusts, universities, medical institutions, supportive-housing providers, and temporary development entities required to transfer completed units.
Section 4 — First-Time Homebuyer Ownership Program

A first-time buyer purchasing a Primary Residence priced at or below $500,000 shall be offered a public ownership contract under which the total required housing payment does not exceed twenty percent of the income of the highest-paid adult permanently residing in the dwelling. The payment shall include principal, financing charges, property taxes, required public assessments, structural loss protection, and unavoidable cooperative charges.
Section 5 — Twenty-Two-Year Conversion to Ownership

After twenty-two years of consecutive required payments, including approved hardship deferrals, the participant shall receive immediate, permanent, unencumbered title. Remaining public principal shall be discharged, Program liens released, and no deferred balloon payment imposed.
Section 6 — Annual Income Adjustment

Payments shall be reviewed annually and may rise or fall with verified income but never above the twenty-percent ceiling. Income decline shall trigger payment reduction, suspension, term extension, employment support, or disability accommodation rather than automatic loss of housing.
Section 7 — Reasonable House Pricing Initiative

Every residence shall receive a Maximum Lawful Transfer Price determined primarily by habitable square footage, structural type, quality, age, condition, energy efficiency, accessibility, documented permanent improvements, regional construction cost, ordinary residential land, and public infrastructure. Market bidding, prestige, scarcity, anticipated appreciation, and expected resale profit shall not independently increase the price.
Section 8 — Tiered Housing Values

Each tier shall have a base construction value, maximum lawful price per square foot, regional cost adjustment, condition and age adjustment, improvement allowance, depreciation schedule, accessibility credit, and energy-efficiency credit. The marginal lawful price per additional square foot may decline in higher tiers.
Section 9 — Prohibition of Residential Resale Profit

A residence may not be sold for financial profit. The maximum resale amount equals the lawful acquisition price plus depreciated approved permanent improvements and unavoidable public assessments, less damage, deterioration, removed improvements, and depreciation, and may never exceed the current lawful transfer price. Interest, taxes, utilities, furnishings, ordinary maintenance, personal labor, and neighborhood demand do not create resale value.
Section 10 — Housing Tier Advancement Credit

An owner transferring a fully paid residence to acquire a higher tier shall receive direct credit for the lawful transfer value against the replacement dwelling. Only the lawful difference remains subject to income-based payment, and prior completed ownership years shall be credited when determining the remaining term.
Section 11 — National Residential Exchange

All residential transfers shall be listed through a transparent National Residential Exchange displaying lawful price, square footage, tier, condition, accessibility, energy performance, and transfer history. Where multiple qualified buyers seek one home, selection shall use a non-price method such as first complete offer, lottery, first-time-buyer priority, accessibility need, household-size compatibility, or essential-worker proximity.
Section 12 — Nonprofit Rental Standard

Residential rental housing shall not be operated for private profit. Lawful providers include public housing authorities, nonprofit organizations, resident cooperatives, community land trusts, educational or medical institutions, supportive-housing providers, and individual homeowners offering a lawful accessory unit without profit.
Section 13 — Lawful Rent and Small-Unit Ceiling

Rent may recover only acquisition or construction principal, maintenance, repair, utilities included, taxes, public assessments, loss protection, administration, reserve funding, accessibility, necessary staffing, and capital replacement. Studios and units not exceeding 1,200 square feet shall not exceed one dollar per square foot per month in base rent. Mandatory occupancy charges shall be included in the stated amount and may not be disguised as amenity, technology, package, parking, renewal, or administrative fees.
Section 14 — Universal Income-Based Rent

Every rental shall offer income-based rent. Total mandatory housing cost shall not exceed twenty percent of the income of the highest-paid adult permanent resident. Where this amount is below actual nonprofit operating cost, the difference shall be supplied by public housing funds, community housing funds, cross-subsidy, or the Protected Human Support Trust.
Section 15 — One Rental Residence

No individual may hold more than one active residential lease or equivalent occupancy right at one time, except for temporary overlap caused by relocation, education, medical care, caregiving, military assignment, separation, disaster, or comparable transition. The overlap shall ordinarily not exceed six months.
Section 16 — Eviction and Foreclosure Protection

No eviction or foreclosure may occur without notice, income reassessment, hardship review, assistance screening, mediation, repayment options, and judicial authorization. A resident shall not be displaced merely to obtain a higher-paying occupant. Government error shall not produce immediate homelessness.
Section 17 — Municipal Minimum Supply Duty

Every locality shall maintain sufficient Tier I and other lowest-tier ownership and rental capacity to satisfy verified local demand. A persistent waiting list, overcrowding, homelessness, displacement, or inability to place qualified residents constitutes a housing deficiency. A municipality may not satisfy its duty merely by directing residents elsewhere.
Section 18 — Commercial Vacancy Conversion Trigger

When lowest-tier demand remains unmet, all substantially unused commercial property shall enter Housing Conversion Review, including vacant plazas, malls, retail stores, offices, hotels, warehouses, schools, institutional buildings, parking structures, and developed commercial land. A property is substantially unused when less than twenty-five percent of usable space supports a genuine operating business for twelve months, absent documented construction, remediation, casualty repair, probate, sale, or funded redevelopment.
Section 19 — Standardized Substantial Living Areas

Converted property shall provide genuine long-term homes with private sleeping space, sanitation, potable water, sewage, heating, climate protection, electricity, ventilation, natural light or approved equivalent, fire separation, exits, food preparation, storage, acoustic privacy, internet capability, accessibility, and durable construction. Commercial conversion shall not create warehouse cubicles or permanent emergency shelters.
Section 20 — Conversion Priority and Designs

The National Housing Authority shall maintain standardized designs for strip plazas, offices, warehouses, malls, hotels, schools, medical buildings, and standalone retail structures. Priority shall consider structural suitability, employment and transit access, utility capacity, safety, environmental condition, unit yield, conversion cost, schedule, and local shortage severity.
Section 21 — Owner Opportunity and Compulsory Acquisition

An owner shall first be offered the opportunity to convert directly under nonprofit rules, transfer to a public or nonprofit body, join a community land trust, or sell at lawful non-speculative value. If the owner declines and verified need persists, compulsory conversion or acquisition may proceed only after notice, independent evaluation, a Housing Conversion Record, opportunity to respond, and judicial approval with lawful compensation.
Section 22 — Mixed-Use Preservation

Conversion should preserve grocery stores, healthcare, childcare, local retail, repair services, small-business workspaces, transit connections, public service offices, green space, community gardens, recreation, energy systems, and other elements needed to transform abandoned commercial land into functioning neighborhoods.
Section 23 — Housing Data and Review

A privacy-protected national database shall track inventory, occupancy, beneficial ownership, rental status, price, tier, condition, vacancy, construction, waiting lists, and transfer history. ONPA shall measure affordability, supply, quality, transfer time, homelessness, ownership concentration, commercial conversion, public cost, and unintended effects.

fullText: Housing shall serve first as a place of residence and human stability rather than an unrestricted vehicle for speculation. This Article establishes limits on residential accumulation, a guaranteed path to first ownership, controlled transfer values, nonprofit rental operation, mandatory income-based affordability, and conversion of unused commercial property when lowest-tier housing demand is unmet.
Section 1 — Residential Ownership Limits
Certified individual annual income Maximum residential properties Below $500,000 One $500,000 through $1,000,000 Two Above $1,000,000 Three

Ownership includes direct, beneficial, trust, corporate, partnership, nominee, or controlling fractional interests. No household may use multiple nominal owners to evade the limits. Temporary exceptions may be granted for inheritance, divorce, replacement construction, relocation, casualty, or estate administration, ordinarily for no more than twenty-four months.
Section 2 — Primary Residence

Every adult may designate only one Primary Residence, which must be actually occupied for most of the year except during temporary absence for medical care, education, military service, work assignment, caregiving, disaster, or comparable necessity.
Section 3 — Prohibition of Corporate Accumulation

For-profit corporations, investment funds, private-equity entities, and similar bodies shall not acquire ordinary homes for passive rent, speculative resale, or appreciation. Permitted institutional ownership includes public housing authorities, nonprofit cooperatives, community land trusts, universities, medical institutions, supportive-housing providers, and temporary development entities required to transfer completed units.
Section 4 — First-Time Homebuyer Ownership Program

A first-time buyer purchasing a Primary Residence priced at or below $500,000 shall be offered a public ownership contract under which the total required housing payment does not exceed twenty percent of the income of the highest-paid adult permanently residing in the dwelling. The payment shall include principal, financing charges, property taxes, required public assessments, structural loss protection, and unavoidable cooperative charges.
Section 5 — Twenty-Two-Year Conversion to Ownership

After twenty-two years of consecutive required payments, including approved hardship deferrals, the participant shall receive immediate, permanent, unencumbered title. Remaining public principal shall be discharged, Program liens released, and no deferred balloon payment imposed.
Section 6 — Annual Income Adjustment

Payments shall be reviewed annually and may rise or fall with verified income but never above the twenty-percent ceiling. Income decline shall trigger payment reduction, suspension, term extension, employment support, or disability accommodation rather than automatic loss of housing.
Section 7 — Reasonable House Pricing Initiative

Every residence shall receive a Maximum Lawful Transfer Price determined primarily by habitable square footage, structural type, quality, age, condition, energy efficiency, accessibility, documented permanent improvements, regional construction cost, ordinary residential land, and public infrastructure. Market bidding, prestige, scarcity, anticipated appreciation, and expected resale profit shall not independently increase the price.
Section 8 — Tiered Housing Values
Tier Habitable area I Up to 600 sq. ft. II 601-1,200 sq. ft. III 1,201-1,800 sq. ft. IV 1,801-2,500 sq. ft. V 2,501-3,500 sq. ft. VI More than 3,500 sq. ft.

Each tier shall have a base construction value, maximum lawful price per square foot, regional cost adjustment, condition and age adjustment, improvement allowance, depreciation schedule, accessibility credit, and energy-efficiency credit. The marginal lawful price per additional square foot may decline in higher tiers.
Section 9 — Prohibition of Residential Resale Profit

A residence may not be sold for financial profit. The maximum resale amount equals the lawful acquisition price plus depreciated approved permanent improvements and unavoidable public assessments, less damage, deterioration, removed improvements, and depreciation, and may never exceed the current lawful transfer price. Interest, taxes, utilities, furnishings, ordinary maintenance, personal labor, and neighborhood demand do not create resale value.
Section 10 — Housing Tier Advancement Credit

An owner transferring a fully paid residence to acquire a higher tier shall receive direct credit for the lawful transfer value against the replacement dwelling. Only the lawful difference remains subject to income-based payment, and prior completed ownership years shall be credited when determining the remaining term.
Section 11 — National Residential Exchange

All residential transfers shall be listed through a transparent National Residential Exchange displaying lawful price, square footage, tier, condition, accessibility, energy performance, and transfer history. Where multiple qualified buyers seek one home, selection shall use a non-price method such as first complete offer, lottery, first-time-buyer priority, accessibility need, household-size compatibility, or essential-worker proximity.
Section 12 — Nonprofit Rental Standard

Residential rental housing shall not be operated for private profit. Lawful providers include public housing authorities, nonprofit organizations, resident cooperatives, community land trusts, educational or medical institutions, supportive-housing providers, and individual homeowners offering a lawful accessory unit without profit.
Section 13 — Lawful Rent and Small-Unit Ceiling

Rent may recover only acquisition or construction principal, maintenance, repair, utilities included, taxes, public assessments, loss protection, administration, reserve funding, accessibility, necessary staffing, and capital replacement. Studios and units not exceeding 1,200 square feet shall not exceed one dollar per square foot per month in base rent. Mandatory occupancy charges shall be included in the stated amount and may not be disguised as amenity, technology, package, parking, renewal, or administrative fees.
Section 14 — Universal Income-Based Rent

Every rental shall offer income-based rent. Total mandatory housing cost shall not exceed twenty percent of the income of the highest-paid adult permanent resident. Where this amount is below actual nonprofit operating cost, the difference shall be supplied by public housing funds, community housing funds, cross-subsidy, or the Protected Human Support Trust.
Section 15 — One Rental Residence

No individual may hold more than one active residential lease or equivalent occupancy right at one time, except for temporary overlap caused by relocation, education, medical care, caregiving, military assignment, separation, disaster, or comparable transition. The overlap shall ordinarily not exceed six months.
Section 16 — Eviction and Foreclosure Protection

No eviction or foreclosure may occur without notice, income reassessment, hardship review, assistance screening, mediation, repayment options, and judicial authorization. A resident shall not be displaced merely to obtain a higher-paying occupant. Government error shall not produce immediate homelessness.
Section 17 — Municipal Minimum Supply Duty

Every locality shall maintain sufficient Tier I and other lowest-tier ownership and rental capacity to satisfy verified local demand. A persistent waiting list, overcrowding, homelessness, displacement, or inability to place qualified residents constitutes a housing deficiency. A municipality may not satisfy its duty merely by directing residents elsewhere.
Section 18 — Commercial Vacancy Conversion Trigger

When lowest-tier demand remains unmet, all substantially unused commercial property shall enter Housing Conversion Review, including vacant plazas, malls, retail stores, offices, hotels, warehouses, schools, institutional buildings, parking structures, and developed commercial land. A property is substantially unused when less than twenty-five percent of usable space supports a genuine operating business for twelve months, absent documented construction, remediation, casualty repair, probate, sale, or funded redevelopment.
Section 19 — Standardized Substantial Living Areas

Converted property shall provide genuine long-term homes with private sleeping space, sanitation, potable water, sewage, heating, climate protection, electricity, ventilation, natural light or approved equivalent, fire separation, exits, food preparation, storage, acoustic privacy, internet capability, accessibility, and durable construction. Commercial conversion shall not create warehouse cubicles or permanent emergency shelters.
Section 20 — Conversion Priority and Designs

The National Housing Authority shall maintain standardized designs for strip plazas, offices, warehouses, malls, hotels, schools, medical buildings, and standalone retail structures. Priority shall consider structural suitability, employment and transit access, utility capacity, safety, environmental condition, unit yield, conversion cost, schedule, and local shortage severity.
Section 21 — Owner Opportunity and Compulsory Acquisition

An owner shall first be offered the opportunity to convert directly under nonprofit rules, transfer to a public or nonprofit body, join a community land trust, or sell at lawful non-speculative value. If the owner declines and verified need persists, compulsory conversion or acquisition may proceed only after notice, independent evaluation, a Housing Conversion Record, opportunity to respond, and judicial approval with lawful compensation.
Section 22 — Mixed-Use Preservation

Conversion should preserve grocery stores, healthcare, childcare, local retail, repair services, small-business workspaces, transit connections, public service offices, green space, community gardens, recreation, energy systems, and other elements needed to transform abandoned commercial land into functioning neighborhoods.
Section 23 — Housing Data and Review

A privacy-protected national database shall track inventory, occupancy, beneficial ownership, rental status, price, tier, condition, vacancy, construction, waiting lists, and transfer history. ONPA shall measure affordability, supply, quality, transfer time, homelessness, ownership concentration, commercial conversion, public cost, and unintended effects.

articles.title: National Rail Revitalization, Mobility Access, and Transportation Independence
articles.summary: The Commonwealth shall construct a modern national railway network capable of replacing a substantial portion of personal-vehicle commuting and short-distance air travel while connecting every state capital, major population center, regional community, and local feeder system. The system shall be affordable, convenient, domestically maintainable, technologically advanced, and usable during national emergencies.

The system shall be affordable, convenient, domestically maintainable, technologically advanced, and usable during national emergencies.
Section 1 — National Rail Authority

An independent National Rail Development and Mobility Authority shall plan corridors, build and modernize infrastructure, establish engineering and safety standards, coordinate state and local systems, certify equipment, maintain public rail data, supervise unified ticketing, manage strategic components, and coordinate domestic manufacturing.
Section 2 — State Capital Hubs

Every state capital shall contain a Major State Rail Hub integrating interstate trunk rail, intrastate regional rail, commuter rail, freight where appropriate, buses, municipal transit, bicycle facilities, pedestrian access, airports, ports, and emergency transport. A hub may be distributed across coordinated stations where a single terminal would create excessive congestion or vulnerability.
Section 3 — Sixteen-Line Junction Standard

textBlocks.text: The Commonwealth shall construct a modern national railway network capable of replacing a substantial portion of personal-vehicle commuting and short-distance air travel while connecting every state capital, major population center, regional community, and local feeder system. The system shall be affordable, convenient, domestically maintainable, technologically advanced, and usable during national emergencies.
Section 1 — National Rail Authority

An independent National Rail Development and Mobility Authority shall plan corridors, build and modernize infrastructure, establish engineering and safety standards, coordinate state and local systems, certify equipment, maintain public rail data, supervise unified ticketing, manage strategic components, and coordinate domestic manufacturing.
Section 2 — State Capital Hubs

Every state capital shall contain a Major State Rail Hub integrating interstate trunk rail, intrastate regional rail, commuter rail, freight where appropriate, buses, municipal transit, bicycle facilities, pedestrian access, airports, ports, and emergency transport. A hub may be distributed across coordinated stations where a single terminal would create excessive congestion or vulnerability.
Section 3 — Sixteen-Line Junction Standard

Each capital hub may contain no more than sixteen principal radial corridors. The corridors shall be geographically balanced while accounting for population, terrain, existing development, employment, universities, hospitals, industry, borders, ports, and environmental constraints. Local branches, station tracks, freight sidings, and feeder services do not count toward the sixteen-line maximum.
Section 4 — Junction and Feeder Network

Principal corridors shall connect to secondary junctions that distribute mobility by regional rail, commuter rail, light rail, tram, bus rapid transit, scheduled bus, rural shuttle, demand-responsive service, bicycle, and safe pedestrian routes. A locality is not served merely because a track passes nearby; practical access and timed connections are required.
Section 5 — Coverage and Redundancy

Every state capital shall be directly connected to the national network. Every major metropolitan area shall receive direct service. Each state should possess at least two independent routes where geography permits, and no major region should depend permanently on one corridor.
Section 6 — Frequency and Convenience

Major corridors shall provide repeated daily service, commuter service aligned to common work shifts, weekend and holiday service, coordinated transfers, night or sleeper service where justified, and reliable timetables. Low-density regions may use shorter trains and flexible feeders rather than being abandoned.
Section 7 — Unified Ticketing and Offline Access

A passenger shall be able to plan and pay for multi-operator travel as one itinerary, one transaction, and one passenger record. Delay protection, transfer rebooking, refunds, accessibility information, and local transit integration shall be automatic. Smartphone ownership shall not be required; cash, physical ticketing, and offline proof of purchase shall remain available.
Section 8 — Affordable Fare Standard

Rail shall operate as essential mobility infrastructure rather than a profit-maximizing monopoly. Fares shall account for distance, service type, income, trip purpose, travel frequency, age, disability, caregiving, and absence of vehicle ownership. Necessary transportation shall not consume an unreasonable share of income.
Section 9 — Vehicle Non-Ownership Mobility Credit

A resident who does not own or beneficially control a personal motor vehicle may receive reduced commuter and intercity fares, free local transfers, capped monthly passes, bicycle carriage, and feeder-service credits. Temporary rental, car-sharing, employer vehicles, medical transport, and occasional borrowing shall not automatically eliminate eligibility.
Section 10 — Purpose-Based Assistance

Reduced or free travel may be provided for employment, job interviews, education, training, medical care, caregiving, court appearances, government services, disability support, emergency relocation, and public service. Verification shall be simple and privacy-protective.
Section 11 — Domestic Production and Open Standards

The Nation shall maintain capacity to design, manufacture, repair, and replace rail, trainsets, locomotives, passenger cars, signals, motors, brakes, power systems, switches, bridges, tunnels, maintenance equipment, software, and critical components. Systems shall use open, interoperable, repairable standards where practical and shall not depend permanently on one foreign or proprietary supplier.
Section 12 — Technology Program

The Agency for the Advancement of Technology shall maintain a Rail Technology Program for high-speed rail, lightweight vehicles, regenerative braking, energy storage, automated inspection, predictive maintenance, collision prevention, resilient communication, low-noise systems, modular stations, tunnel boring, climate resilience, cybersecurity, and manual fallback operation.
Section 13 — Energy and Continuity

Electrification, distributed generation, station microgrids, regenerative braking, storage, segmented power, backup energy, black-start capability, independent communications, manual dispatch, reserve crews, mobile maintenance, alternate routes, and spare components shall allow critical corridors to function during grid, internet, fuel, and supply-chain disruptions.
Section 14 — Station-Centered Communities

Housing, employment, childcare, healthcare, education, groceries, public services, local business, pedestrian paths, and bicycle infrastructure shall be concentrated near stations. Rail investment shall not become a device for speculative housing profit and shall prioritize lowest-tier housing and nonprofit occupancy.
Section 15 — Construction Phases

The program shall proceed through mapping and corridor preservation; connection of every state capital; regional junction expansion; high-speed and capacity construction; accessibility retrofit; and universal mobility completion. Project priority shall be based on population served, traffic reduction, access, travel time, feasibility, emergency value, geographic equity, and public benefit rather than political influence.
Section 16 — Passenger Rights and Fare Continuity

Passengers possess rights to clear fares, safe and accessible travel, delay information, automatic compensation, privacy, human review of automated enforcement, and non-regression of purchased fare plans. A fare tier may not be degraded after enrollment, and higher tiers must provide genuine additional value.
Section 17 — National Action Records and Review

Every major corridor, hub, high-speed project, fare policy, route discontinuation, acquisition, and construction phase requires a National Rail Action Record. ONPA shall measure ridership, cost, reliability, coverage, traffic reduction, domestic production, energy use, safety, accessibility, housing effects, delays, and cost overruns.

articles.title: National Education, Child Development, Productive Learning, and School Security
articles.summary: Education shall prepare every child for competent adulthood, scientific and technological participation, skilled work, civic responsibility, personal independence, creativity, physical and mental health, and continued learning. Schools shall function as secure, welcoming, full-workday institutions that support working families without reducing childhood to continuous formal instruction.

excerpt: Education shall prepare every child for competent adulthood, scientific and technological participation, skilled work, civic responsibility, personal independence, creativity, physical and mental health, and continued learning. Schools shall function as secure, welcoming, full-workday institutions that support working families without reducing childhood to continuous formal instruction.
Section 1 — Universal Educational Access

Public education, transportation, meals, required supplies, ordinary technology, and workday childcare shall be provided without tuition. Access shall not depend on family wealth, property, disability, geography, family structure, parent citizenship, academic performance, or ability to provide daytime supervision.
Section 2 — Developmental Structure

Education shall progress through Early Childhood, Foundation, Exploratory STEM, and Advanced Preparation stages. Early stages emphasize language, numeracy, play, health, emotional regulation, and curiosity; middle stages introduce laboratory science, programming, engineering, design, data, and trades; later stages provide advanced mathematics, sciences, technical writing, career pathways, skilled trades, research, civics, and elective arts and humanities.

textBlocks.text: Education shall prepare every child for competent adulthood, scientific and technological participation, skilled work, civic responsibility, personal independence, creativity, physical and mental health, and continued learning. Schools shall function as secure, welcoming, full-workday institutions that support working families without reducing childhood to continuous formal instruction.
Section 1 — Universal Educational Access

Public education, transportation, meals, required supplies, ordinary technology, and workday childcare shall be provided without tuition. Access shall not depend on family wealth, property, disability, geography, family structure, parent citizenship, academic performance, or ability to provide daytime supervision.
Section 2 — Developmental Structure

Education shall progress through Early Childhood, Foundation, Exploratory STEM, and Advanced Preparation stages. Early stages emphasize language, numeracy, play, health, emotional regulation, and curiosity; middle stages introduce laboratory science, programming, engineering, design, data, and trades; later stages provide advanced mathematics, sciences, technical writing, career pathways, skilled trades, research, civics, and elective arts and humanities.
Section 3 — STEM Priority and Arts Foundation

Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics shall be the primary academic concentration of compulsory education. Every child shall receive arithmetic, algebra, geometry, statistics, biology, chemistry, physics, earth science, programming, digital systems, engineering design, data interpretation, AI literacy, cybersecurity awareness, experimentation, and repair. Arts shall remain required as a secondary foundation through visual art, music, design, writing, performance, media, architecture, craft, and creative problem-solving.
Section 4 — Higher Education Choice

At higher education, students may elect any legitimate field, including arts, humanities, social sciences, law, education, philosophy, languages, history, public administration, science, engineering, medicine, and trades. Public investment may prioritize national shortages without eliminating choice.
Section 5 — Practical Competencies

Graduation shall require demonstrated competence in reading contracts, finance, taxes, employment applications, workplace communication, first aid, food preparation, digital security, personal-data protection, basic repair, transportation systems, civics, emergency preparation, conflict resolution, and evaluation of evidence and misinformation.
Section 6 — Full Workday Availability

Every school shall remain available long enough to encompass a standard work shift and ordinary commuting time, generally nine to eleven supervised hours. The extended day shall combine instruction, meals, physical activity, tutoring, projects, recreation, clubs, arts, quiet study, and rest. Separate staffing structures shall prevent teachers from being required to supervise the entire extended period.
Section 7 — Transportation Without Routine Drop-Off

Every student shall receive public school transportation. Routine parent drop-off and pickup shall not be the primary system. Students shall ordinarily travel by school bus, approved transit, walking, bicycle, or specialized accessible transport. Direct parent transport remains available for medical need, disability, emergency, transport failure, specialized placement, remote residence, or comparable hardship.
Section 8 — Universal Workday Childcare

Every district shall provide or coordinate no-cost care for children below compulsory school age when needed during a parent or guardian’s verified work, training, education, employment search, public service, medical treatment, court attendance, commute, or emergency. Care shall include qualified staffing, age-appropriate ratios, nutrition, rest, play, development, health procedures, disability accommodation, and secure release.
Section 9 — School Security Principle

Schools shall receive the highest practical security consistent with a friendly, developmentally appropriate, non-carceral environment. Security shall emphasize controlled access, early threat identification, de-escalation, mental-health intervention, emergency response, evacuation, and family reunification, without treating ordinary children as criminal suspects.
Section 10 — Continuous Monitoring and Protective Staffing

School grounds and critical access points shall be monitored twenty-four hours per day through trained personnel, cameras, alarms, environmental sensors, lighting, communications, and patrols. While students are present, each campus shall maintain at least three trained protective officers, with additional staffing based on size, layout, population, and emergency-response time.
Section 11 — Retired Veteran School Protection Corps

A civilian Retired Veteran School Protection Corps may recruit honorably discharged or retired veterans who pass comprehensive background, psychological, medical, child-safety, de-escalation, youth-interaction, emergency-medical, less-lethal equipment, and legal-use-of-force training. Veteran status alone is not qualification. Officers shall not enforce ordinary classroom discipline or create a militarized culture.
Section 12 — Defensive Equipment and Force

Protective officers may carry nationally approved less-lethal equipment, including pepper-projectile launchers, defensive spray, shields, restraint equipment, and emergency medical gear. Such devices shall be governed as capable of serious injury. Force is permitted only to prevent imminent injury, violent entry, assault, abduction, or comparable danger and never for truancy, disrespect, passive noncompliance, ordinary disruption, punishment, or intimidation.
Section 13 — Controlled Access and Badges

School buildings shall require unique credentials for students, staff, contractors, guardians, and visitors, with visitor registration, temporary badges, recorded entry and exit, and emergency override. Badges may support entry, transportation, meals, attendance, libraries, equipment, and reunification but shall not enable unrestricted behavioral tracking, commercial use, or indefinite data retention.
Section 14 — Friendly Secure Design

New and renovated schools shall combine welcoming entrances, natural light, landscaped grounds, comfortable common areas, child-scaled interiors, safe outdoor recreation, and quiet spaces with controlled access, secure classroom doors, compartmentalized emergency zones, protected exits, communications, backup power, independent water and sanitation, medical response areas, and responder access.
Section 15 — Mental Health and Threat Prevention

Every school shall maintain a multidisciplinary Student Safety and Support Team and access to counselors, psychologists, social workers, crisis intervention, suicide prevention, trauma care, substance-use support, bullying intervention, family referral, and emergency psychiatric care. Security personnel shall not replace mental-health professionals.
Section 16 — Productive Education and Child Labor Reform

Schools may reserve a limited portion of the week for supervised, age-appropriate, low-risk productive education. The purpose is to teach reliability, punctuality, quality, cooperation, sanitation, safe tool use, inventory, planning, workplace communication, respect for labor, and the relation between effort and community benefit. Student labor shall never replace employees, reduce labor cost, fulfill private commercial contracts, or generate private profit.
Section 17 — Permitted Productive Activities

Programs may include baking, school meal preparation, safe food packaging, gardening, greenhouse work, sewing and textile repair, basic woodworking, bicycle repair, electronics disassembly and supervised repair, material sorting, printing, binding, school supplies, emergency kits, library and equipment inventory, digital production, community meals, and other approved low-risk work. Each school or regional cooperative shall maintain at least one locally sourced productive program where feasible.
Section 18 — Time, Rest, and Prohibited Work

Below the Advanced Preparation Stage, productive work shall ordinarily not exceed forty-five minutes in one day or two hours in one week. Older students shall ordinarily not exceed ninety minutes in one day, five hours in one week, or ten percent of total instructional time. Work must include breaks and rotation. Hazardous chemicals, heavy machinery, high voltage, dangerous heights, firearms, toxic waste, slaughter, excessive lifting, late-night work, confidential records, employee replacement, and private political or religious labor are prohibited.
Section 19 — Student Benefit and Compensation

Produced value shall return to students through meals, materials, equipment, activity funds, scholarships, savings accounts, community support, or reduced school cost. Sales revenue shall enter a protected Student Productive Education Fund. Older students performing advanced or sustained productive work may receive direct compensation and educational credit.
Section 20 — Opt-Out and Alternative Electives

A student or parent may opt out without explanation or penalty. The school shall provide a meaningful alternative elective in STEM, arts, music, reading, coding, research, finance, civics, language, or independent study. Parents may be responsible for special elective materials beyond standard provision, but inability to pay shall never exclude a child; the school must offer a no-cost alternative or assistance.
Section 21 — Teachers, Technical Instructors, and Facilities

Teachers shall be treated as high-skill professionals. A STEM Instructor Corps may recruit engineers, scientists, programmers, clinicians, mathematicians, tradespeople, researchers, and technical retirees after instructional and child-safety training. Every secondary school shall have access to laboratories, computing, fabrication, electronics, robotics, repair spaces, technical drawing, protective equipment, and high-speed and offline resources.
Section 22 — Assessment, Competency, and Pathways

Assessment shall combine examinations, laboratories, projects, oral explanation, engineering demonstrations, portfolios, team problem-solving, certifications, apprenticeships, and research. Students may advance by competency and select pathways in engineering, computing, medicine, physical sciences, mathematics, trades, manufacturing, agriculture, business, education, arts, humanities, public safety, or other fields without permanent tracking.
Section 23 — Continuity and Community Use

Schools shall maintain plans for power, internet, water, transport, weather, violence, pandemic, fire, building closure, and Survival Mode. They may serve as community continuity centers for shelter, food, water, medical support, communication, childcare, charging, family reunification, and local government support.
Section 24 — Funding and Review

Education shall receive priority funding sufficient for staff, transportation, childcare, meals, security, mental health, laboratories, arts, trades, special education, extended-day operation, rural access, and maintenance. ONPA shall measure literacy, mathematics, science, technical competence, safety, childcare access, family work stability, teacher retention, security outcomes, student privacy, work-program safety, and long-term quality of life.

At higher education, students may elect any legitimate field, including arts, humanities, social sciences, law, education, philosophy, languages, history, public administration, science, engineering, medicine, and trades. Public investment may prioritize national shortages without eliminating choice.
Section 5 — Practical Competencies

Graduation shall require demonstrated competence in reading contracts, finance, taxes, employment applications, workplace communication, first aid, food preparation, digital security, personal-data protection, basic repair, transportation systems, civics, emergency preparation, conflict resolution, and evaluation of evidence and misinformation.
Section 6 — Full Workday Availability

Every school shall remain available long enough to encompass a standard work shift and ordinary commuting time, generally nine to eleven supervised hours. The extended day shall combine instruction, meals, physical activity, tutoring, projects, recreation, clubs, arts, quiet study, and rest. Separate staffing structures shall prevent teachers from being required to supervise the entire extended period.
Section 7 — Transportation Without Routine Drop-Off

Every student shall receive public school transportation. Routine parent drop-off and pickup shall not be the primary system. Students shall ordinarily travel by school bus, approved transit, walking, bicycle, or specialized accessible transport. Direct parent transport remains available for medical need, disability, emergency, transport failure, specialized placement, remote residence, or comparable hardship.
Section 8 — Universal Workday Childcare

Every district shall provide or coordinate no-cost care for children below compulsory school age when needed during a parent or guardian’s verified work, training, education, employment search, public service, medical treatment, court attendance, commute, or emergency. Care shall include qualified staffing, age-appropriate ratios, nutrition, rest, play, development, health procedures, disability accommodation, and secure release.
Section 9 — School Security Principle

Schools shall receive the highest practical security consistent with a friendly, developmentally appropriate, non-carceral environment. Security shall emphasize controlled access, early threat identification, de-escalation, mental-health intervention, emergency response, evacuation, and family reunification, without treating ordinary children as criminal suspects.
Section 10 — Continuous Monitoring and Protective Staffing

School grounds and critical access points shall be monitored twenty-four hours per day through trained personnel, cameras, alarms, environmental sensors, lighting, communications, and patrols. While students are present, each campus shall maintain at least three trained protective officers, with additional staffing based on size, layout, population, and emergency-response time.
Section 11 — Retired Veteran School Protection Corps

A civilian Retired Veteran School Protection Corps may recruit honorably discharged or retired veterans who pass comprehensive background, psychological, medical, child-safety, de-escalation, youth-interaction, emergency-medical, less-lethal equipment, and legal-use-of-force training. Veteran status alone is not qualification. Officers shall not enforce ordinary classroom discipline or create a militarized culture.
Section 12 — Defensive Equipment and Force

Protective officers may carry nationally approved less-lethal equipment, including pepper-projectile launchers, defensive spray, shields, restraint equipment, and emergency medical gear. Such devices shall be governed as capable of serious injury. Force is permitted only to prevent imminent injury, violent entry, assault, abduction, or comparable danger and never for truancy, disrespect, passive noncompliance, ordinary disruption, punishment, or intimidation.
Section 13 — Controlled Access and Badges

School buildings shall require unique credentials for students, staff, contractors, guardians, and visitors, with visitor registration, temporary badges, recorded entry and exit, and emergency override. Badges may support entry, transportation, meals, attendance, libraries, equipment, and reunification but shall not enable unrestricted behavioral tracking, commercial use, or indefinite data retention.
Section 14 — Friendly Secure Design

New and renovated schools shall combine welcoming entrances, natural light, landscaped grounds, comfortable common areas, child-scaled interiors, safe outdoor recreation, and quiet spaces with controlled access, secure classroom doors, compartmentalized emergency zones, protected exits, communications, backup power, independent water and sanitation, medical response areas, and responder access.
Section 15 — Mental Health and Threat Prevention

Every school shall maintain a multidisciplinary Student Safety and Support Team and access to counselors, psychologists, social workers, crisis intervention, suicide prevention, trauma care, substance-use support, bullying intervention, family referral, and emergency psychiatric care. Security personnel shall not replace mental-health professionals.
Section 16 — Productive Education and Child Labor Reform

Schools may reserve a limited portion of the week for supervised, age-appropriate, low-risk productive education. The purpose is to teach reliability, punctuality, quality, cooperation, sanitation, safe tool use, inventory, planning, workplace communication, respect for labor, and the relation between effort and community benefit. Student labor shall never replace employees, reduce labor cost, fulfill private commercial contracts, or generate private profit.
Section 17 — Permitted Productive Activities

Programs may include baking, school meal preparation, safe food packaging, gardening, greenhouse work, sewing and textile repair, basic woodworking, bicycle repair, electronics disassembly and supervised repair, material sorting, printing, binding, school supplies, emergency kits, library and equipment inventory, digital production, community meals, and other approved low-risk work. Each school or regional cooperative shall maintain at least one locally sourced productive program where feasible.
Section 18 — Time, Rest, and Prohibited Work

Below the Advanced Preparation Stage, productive work shall ordinarily not exceed forty-five minutes in one day or two hours in one week. Older students shall ordinarily not exceed ninety minutes in one day, five hours in one week, or ten percent of total instructional time. Work must include breaks and rotation. Hazardous chemicals, heavy machinery, high voltage, dangerous heights, firearms, toxic waste, slaughter, excessive lifting, late-night work, confidential records, employee replacement, and private political or religious labor are prohibited.
Section 19 — Student Benefit and Compensation

Produced value shall return to students through meals, materials, equipment, activity funds, scholarships, savings accounts, community support, or reduced school cost. Sales revenue shall enter a protected Student Productive Education Fund. Older students performing advanced or sustained productive work may receive direct compensation and educational credit.
Section 20 — Opt-Out and Alternative Electives

A student or parent may opt out without explanation or penalty. The school shall provide a meaningful alternative elective in STEM, arts, music, reading, coding, research, finance, civics, language, or independent study. Parents may be responsible for special elective materials beyond standard provision, but inability to pay shall never exclude a child; the school must offer a no-cost alternative or assistance.
Section 21 — Teachers, Technical Instructors, and Facilities

Teachers shall be treated as high-skill professionals. A STEM Instructor Corps may recruit engineers, scientists, programmers, clinicians, mathematicians, tradespeople, researchers, and technical retirees after instructional and child-safety training. Every secondary school shall have access to laboratories, computing, fabrication, electronics, robotics, repair spaces, technical drawing, protective equipment, and high-speed and offline resources.
Section 22 — Assessment, Competency, and Pathways

Assessment shall combine examinations, laboratories, projects, oral explanation, engineering demonstrations, portfolios, team problem-solving, certifications, apprenticeships, and research. Students may advance by competency and select pathways in engineering, computing, medicine, physical sciences, mathematics, trades, manufacturing, agriculture, business, education, arts, humanities, public safety, or other fields without permanent tracking.
Section 23 — Continuity and Community Use

Schools shall maintain plans for power, internet, water, transport, weather, violence, pandemic, fire, building closure, and Survival Mode. They may serve as community continuity centers for shelter, food, water, medical support, communication, childcare, charging, family reunification, and local government support.
Section 24 — Funding and Review

Education shall receive priority funding sufficient for staff, transportation, childcare, meals, security, mental health, laboratories, arts, trades, special education, extended-day operation, rural access, and maintenance. ONPA shall measure literacy, mathematics, science, technical competence, safety, childcare access, family work stability, teacher retention, security outcomes, student privacy, work-program safety, and long-term quality of life.

articles.title: National Health Intelligence, De-Identified Medical Data, and Preventive Care
articles.summary: The Commonwealth shall aggregate de-identified medical knowledge at national scale to improve diagnosis, treatment, prevention, mental health, public-health readiness, and reduction of comorbidities without creating a named national medical surveillance system.

Section 1 — National Health Intelligence Network

A distributed National Health Intelligence Network shall support diagnostic accuracy, treatment comparison, adverse-effect detection, rare-disease identification, comorbidity research, public-health planning, prevention, rehabilitation, physical health, mental health, and development of new medical methodologies.
Section 2 — No Names and Minimal Identifiers

The national analytical system shall not contain names, addresses, telephone numbers, email addresses, government identifiers, financial accounts, employer or school identity, photographs, facial-recognition data, family names, or unnecessary biometrics. The most identifying ordinary fields shall be date of birth, blood type, medically relevant biological characteristics, general geographic region, and a protected nonpublic record identifier.

textBlocks.text: The Commonwealth shall aggregate de-identified medical knowledge at national scale to improve diagnosis, treatment, prevention, mental health, public-health readiness, and reduction of comorbidities without creating a named national medical surveillance system.
Section 1 — National Health Intelligence Network

A distributed National Health Intelligence Network shall support diagnostic accuracy, treatment comparison, adverse-effect detection, rare-disease identification, comorbidity research, public-health planning, prevention, rehabilitation, physical health, mental health, and development of new medical methodologies.
Section 2 — No Names and Minimal Identifiers

The national analytical system shall not contain names, addresses, telephone numbers, email addresses, government identifiers, financial accounts, employer or school identity, photographs, facial-recognition data, family names, or unnecessary biometrics. The most identifying ordinary fields shall be date of birth, blood type, medically relevant biological characteristics, general geographic region, and a protected nonpublic record identifier.
Section 3 — Birthdate Protection

Exact birthdate may exist in a protected core when medically necessary. Researchers shall ordinarily receive only age, age band, month and year, year, or developmental stage. Exact birthdate combined with rare disease, blood type, geography, genetics, or treatment history shall be treated as potentially identifying.
Section 4 — Protected Identifier and Separate Linkage

Each record shall use a cryptographically generated identifier that reveals no identity, provider, state, or government number and permits longitudinal analysis. The identity linkage shall remain outside the national analytical database with originating institutions or state custodians under separate security controls.
Section 5 — State Health Data Nodes and Backups

Each state shall maintain a State Health Data Node that receives provider data, removes direct identifiers, validates quality, generates identifiers, performs state analysis, corrects errors, and synchronizes nationally. Each state shall maintain at least one physically and digitally independent backup, geographically separated, independently powered, restorable offline, and capable of operation during national disconnection.
Section 6 — Distributed Architecture

The Network shall consist of state nodes, a national analytical layer, geographically distributed backups, secure research environments, isolated emergency systems, offline restoration, versioned datasets, complete logs, open technical standards, and redundant communication. No unrestricted central database shall contain every identifiable record.
Section 7 — Permitted Medical Data

The Network may include diagnoses, symptoms, laboratories, imaging findings, vital signs, medication, treatment, surgery, allergy, adverse reaction, genetics under heightened protection, family risk patterns without names, mental health, substance-use treatment, disability, pregnancy, reproductive health, vaccination, rehabilitation, devices, outcomes, hospitalization, exposure, and cause of death where relevant. Collection shall remain limited to legitimate health purposes.
Section 8 — Research Access

Researchers shall define the question, methodology, minimum data, security, ethics, conflicts, duration, deletion, and publication purpose. Access shall ordinarily occur inside controlled environments rather than by unrestricted download. Every access shall be logged by user, institution, data, purpose, time, export, and resulting publication.
Section 9 — Prohibition of Re-Identification and Commercial Sale

No person may attempt to identify an individual from de-identified data except for a narrowly authorized immediate medical-safety purpose. Data shall not be sold, used for advertising, employment, insurance pricing, housing, credit, political targeting, ordinary law enforcement, immigration enforcement, protest monitoring, or commercial profiling.
Section 10 — Mental Health and Genetic Protections

Mental-health and genetic data shall receive heightened controls. They shall not be used to restrict voting, speech, employment, housing, credit, insurance, ordinary travel, or civic status; to identify families without consent; or to support involuntary treatment without separate individualized legal process.
Section 11 — Comorbidity Reduction Program

The Network shall identify combinations of physical, mental, environmental, behavioral, and social conditions that produce disability, hospitalization, premature death, or reduced quality of life. Priority areas include metabolic and cardiovascular disease, depression with chronic illness, addiction with mental illness, chronic pain with psychiatric conditions, respiratory disease with environmental exposure, dementia risk, medication interactions, and pregnancy complications.
Section 12 — Diagnostic, Treatment, and Preventive Advancement

National data shall support earlier detection, differential diagnosis, rare-disease recognition, laboratory and imaging interpretation, medication-response prediction, reduction of misdiagnosis, comparison of medicines and procedures, rehabilitation outcomes, lifestyle interventions, mental-health and addiction treatment, screening, nutrition, sleep, workplace safety, maternal health, elder health, and injury prevention.
Section 13 — Artificial Intelligence

AI may discover patterns and support clinical decisions only after documented data review, bias testing, clinical validation, error measurement, auditability, and human oversight. AI shall distinguish correlation from causation and may not possess final authority to deny care, confine a person, terminate benefits, or establish criminal responsibility.
Section 14 — Patient Rights

Every person has the right to plain-language notice, knowledge of categories collected, correction of inaccurate data, access audit, breach notice, complaint, remedy, and equal care regardless of data status. Optional research involving identifiable contact, samples, experiments, or direct participation requires informed consent.
Section 15 — Cybersecurity and Breach Response

The Network shall use encryption, segmentation, least privilege, multi-factor authentication, intrusion detection, independent penetration testing, offline backups, physical security, supply-chain review, immutable logs, incident response, and key rotation. Serious breach concealment is a constitutional offense.
Section 16 — Oversight and Measurement

A National Health Data Council shall include clinicians, mental-health professionals, epidemiologists, researchers, statisticians, cybersecurity and privacy experts, patients, disability representatives, state officials, legal scholars, ethicists, and students or early-career researchers. ONPA shall measure diagnostic improvement, treatment outcomes, adverse events, comorbidities, hospitalization, research productivity, privacy breaches, re-identification attempts, public trust, cost, and unintended harm.

articles.title: Credit Card Regulation, Consumer Protection, and Debt Relief
articles.summary: Credit cards shall operate as limited payment and short-term borrowing tools, not as permanent systems for extracting interest from consumers unable to reduce principal. This Article prohibits youth revolving debt, unfair interest, manipulative targeting, hidden fees, and indefinite minimum-payment structures while guaranteeing a standardized interest-free repayment path.
sections: Section 4 — Unfair Interest and Penalty Rates
Section 7 — Account Freeze and Recurring Charges

excerpt: Credit cards shall operate as limited payment and short-term borrowing tools, not as permanent systems for extracting interest from consumers unable to reduce principal. This Article prohibits youth revolving debt, unfair interest, manipulative targeting, hidden fees, and indefinite minimum-payment structures while guaranteeing a standardized interest-free repayment path.
Section 1 — Minimum Age

No person under twenty-five may open, jointly hold, guarantee, or become legally liable on a consumer credit-card account. Persons under twenty-five may use debit, prepaid accounts, non-revolving secured products, or an authorized-user card carrying no personal liability. Campus solicitation and youth-directed marketing are prohibited.
Section 2 — Ability-to-Repay Underwriting

Issuers shall verify individual income, existing debt, essential housing cost, court obligations, current limits, payment history, and reasonable emergency expenses. Approval may not be based primarily on predicted interest or fee revenue, and household income may not be attributed without a legal and reliable right of access.
Section 3 — Interest-Rate Ceiling

textBlocks.text: Credit cards shall operate as limited payment and short-term borrowing tools, not as permanent systems for extracting interest from consumers unable to reduce principal. This Article prohibits youth revolving debt, unfair interest, manipulative targeting, hidden fees, and indefinite minimum-payment structures while guaranteeing a standardized interest-free repayment path.
Section 1 — Minimum Age

No person under twenty-five may open, jointly hold, guarantee, or become legally liable on a consumer credit-card account. Persons under twenty-five may use debit, prepaid accounts, non-revolving secured products, or an authorized-user card carrying no personal liability. Campus solicitation and youth-directed marketing are prohibited.
Section 2 — Ability-to-Repay Underwriting

Issuers shall verify individual income, existing debt, essential housing cost, court obligations, current limits, payment history, and reasonable emergency expenses. Approval may not be based primarily on predicted interest or fee revenue, and household income may not be attributed without a legal and reliable right of access.
Section 3 — Interest-Rate Ceiling

The ordinary annual percentage rate shall not exceed the national short-term public lending rate plus eight percentage points and shall never exceed fifteen percent. Interest-equivalent membership, management, risk, processing, service, or required protection charges are included in the ceiling.
Section 4 — Unfair Interest and Penalty Rates

Penalty interest, retroactive rate increases, interest on fees, double-cycle billing, undisclosed calculations, and interest during an approved hardship suspension are prohibited. Total interest and fees on a defined principal balance shall not exceed fifty percent of the original principal; after that threshold, all payments reduce principal.
Section 5 — Minimum Payment Standard

Minimum payments shall include current lawful interest and fees plus enough principal to retire the balance within seven years if no new transactions occur. Statements shall show the current minimum, the payment and total cost for two-year and five-year payoff, and the projected payoff date.
Section 6 — Twenty-Four-Month Interest-Free Benefit

Any consumer carrying a balance may activate a twenty-four-month interest-free plan by paying ten percent of the total posted balance and ceasing all use of the card. Interest and program fees stop immediately. The remaining balance is divided into equal monthly principal payments over twenty-four months, with faster payment permitted without penalty.
Section 7 — Account Freeze and Recurring Charges

The issuer shall freeze the physical card, digital wallets, stored merchant credentials, convenience checks, authorized-user cards, cash advances, transfers, and replacement cards. Before enrollment, the issuer shall identify recurring charges and allow a reasonable period for migration. The issuer may not permit new use and later blame the consumer for failing to freeze the account.
Section 8 — Hardship and Missed Payments

Verified unemployment, medical incapacity, disability, caregiving, disaster, deployment, or comparable hardship may extend the plan by up to twelve months without interest. One late or missed payment does not terminate protection; notice, a thirty-day cure, adjustment, and hardship review are required. Interest may never be imposed retroactively for the protected period.
Section 9 — Completion and Reporting

Upon completion, the account is paid in full, no deferred interest exists, and the account closes unless the consumer separately applies for new credit. Credit reporting shall identify successful completion of an approved repayment plan rather than default, settlement, or charge-off.
Section 10 — Targeted Promotion Prohibition

Issuers shall not target offers using unemployment, medical debt, housing instability, overdrafts, emergency benefits, mental-health or substance-use treatment, divorce, bereavement, gambling, payday-loan use, minimum-payment behavior, declining scores, student status, age below twenty-five, emergency searches, or other evidence of desperation or impaired judgment. Consumers meeting the same published criteria shall receive substantially equivalent terms.
Section 11 — Introductory Offers, Transfers, and Rewards

Promotions shall disclose the exact expiration, ordinary rate, repayment needed within the promotional period, fees, and conditions. Deferred interest is prohibited. Payments above the minimum apply first to the highest-rate balance. Rewards may not require carrying debt, and earned rewards shall not be confiscated merely because a consumer enters hardship or repayment.
Section 12 — Fees and Data

Inactivity, account closure, repayment enrollment, payment-by-phone, online payment, statement access, limit increase, declined transaction, and duplicate late fees are prohibited. Purchase history shall not be sold or used to target harmful financial products. Medical, political, religious, location, or behavioral data shall not determine price.
Section 13 — Collection and Debt Sale

Collectors may not threaten arrest, disclose debt, humiliate, contact excessively, misstate amounts, or pressure medically incapacitated consumers without accommodation. A debt buyer acquires no greater rights than the issuer and must possess complete agreements, payment, interest, fee, dispute, and ownership documentation. Undocumented debt is unenforceable.
Section 14 — National Awareness Campaign

A permanent, publicly funded, industry-independent campaign shall explain revolving debt, compounding, minimum-payment traps, fees, promotions, balance transfers, credit scores, collection, fraud, alternatives, and the twenty-four-month benefit. Before receiving a first card at age twenty-five, an applicant shall complete a free standardized education module. Secondary schools shall teach practical debt calculations without credit-industry sponsorship.
Section 15 — Enforcement and Review

Remedies include cancellation of unlawful interest and fees, balance recalculation, refunds, damages, interest-free repayment, reporting correction, suspension of account issuance, promotion restrictions, executive clawbacks, probation, blacklisting, revocation of issuing authority, and prosecution for fraud. ONPA shall measure debt, interest, fees, payoff time, bankruptcy, education, plan use, youth exposure, promotion violations, availability of responsible credit, and movement toward unregulated lending.

articles.title: National Circular Sanitation, Water Conservation, and Elimination of Conventional Water-Carried Sewage
articles.summary: Clean water is a critical national resource necessary for drinking, food production, public health, medicine, agriculture, industry, ecological stability, emergency survival, and long-term national security.
sections: Section 11 — Human Waste as a Recoverable Resource
Section 12 — Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities
Section 18 — Agricultural Use of Recovered Materials
Section 29 — National Sanitation Technology Challenge Program
Section 48 — Public Ownership and Utility Operation
Section 61 — Small Communities and Rural Areas
excerpt: Section 1 — National Principle

Clean water is a critical national resource necessary for drinking, food production, public health, medicine, agriculture, industry, ecological stability, emergency survival, and long-term national security.

The routine use of large quantities of potable or otherwise reusable water primarily to transport human waste shall be recognized as an inefficient and ultimately unsustainable infrastructure practice.

The Nation shall progressively replace the conventional model of flushing, dilution, long-distance transport, centralized treatment, and environmental discharge with a circular sanitation system based upon:

items: clean water shall no longer be routinely consumed merely to transport human waste;

greywater shall be separated, treated, and reused wherever practical;
industrial and hazardous waste shall be prevented from contaminating public biological recovery systems;
nutrients and energy shall be recovered rather than discharged;
