Section 3 — National Circular Sanitation Authority
There shall exist an independent National Circular Sanitation Authority.
The Authority shall oversee the national transition and shall possess responsibility for:

items: Coordinating states, municipalities, utilities, industries, public institutions, and research bodies.
Certifying Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities.
Supporting technical education and workforce development.
Coordinating with the Office of National Performance and Accountability.

textBlocks.text: The Authority shall not exercise unrestricted control over local sanitation design.

Local and regional systems may differ according to population density, geography, climate, public-health needs, existing infrastructure, water availability, soil conditions, industrial activity, and community preference, provided national safety and performance standards are satisfied.
Section 4 — Public-Health Priority
Public health shall remain the first operational requirement of sanitation infrastructure.
No reduction in water use, decentralization, resource recovery, cost savings, or technological experimentation shall justify:

items: or the denial of sanitary services.

textBlocks.text: Every sanitation system shall provide containment, collection, treatment, monitoring, maintenance, and emergency response sufficient to prevent disease and environmental harm.
The transition shall proceed only as quickly as safe infrastructure, trained personnel, replacement components, public understanding, and verified treatment capacity permit.
Section 5 — Separation of Waste Streams
The national sanitation system shall progressively separate waste streams that are unnecessarily mixed under conventional sewer systems.
Separate management shall be established for:

items: Other specialized or persistent waste streams.

textBlocks.text: The purpose of separation shall be to prevent dilution, contamination, treatment difficulty, resource loss, and unnecessary energy consumption.
Materials shall not be combined where mixing materially reduces recoverability, increases risk, or transfers specialized treatment costs to the public.
Section 6 — New Construction Standard
New buildings shall progressively adopt approved sanitation technologies capable of substantially reducing or eliminating potable-water use for waste transportation.
Approved systems may include:

items: Composting systems meeting national health standards.
Other technologies certified by the Authority.

textBlocks.text: No one technology shall be imposed nationally where multiple systems can satisfy the required performance standard.

The governing standard shall be measured performance, including water conservation, safety, odor control, pathogen destruction, maintenance, repairability, lifecycle cost, resource recovery, and user dignity.
Section 7 — Progressive Construction Requirements
The Authority shall establish phased requirements for new construction.
The initial phases shall require:

items: Separate greywater plumbing in large new buildings.
Plumbing layouts capable of future source separation.
Ultra-low-water or vacuum systems in designated water-stressed regions.
Sanitation equipment capable of repair and replacement without major structural demolition.
Accessible service points for collection and maintenance.
Prohibition of unnecessary mixing between sanitary, storm, and industrial systems.
Metering sufficient to measure water use and recovery without creating personal surveillance.
Space for recovery, storage, or connection to neighborhood systems.

textBlocks.text: Later phases shall require approved circular sanitation systems in new residential, commercial, institutional, industrial, and public construction where such systems are technically feasible.
Section 8 — Major Renovations
A major renovation shall trigger review of the building’s sanitation system.
Where technically and economically reasonable, major renovations shall include:

items: and connection to neighborhood or municipal recovery infrastructure.

textBlocks.text: A building shall not be required to undergo structural reconstruction grossly disproportionate to the renovation merely to satisfy a later-stage requirement.
However, renovations shall not deliberately preserve obsolete infrastructure where integration of modern sanitation systems is reasonably practical.
Section 9 — Greywater Recovery
Greywater shall be treated as a recoverable water resource.
Greywater from bathing, handwashing, laundry, and other approved low-contaminant uses shall be separated from human waste and hazardous wastewater wherever technically practical.
After treatment appropriate to its intended use, greywater may be reused for:
Potable reuse may occur only after treatment and verification meeting national drinking-water standards.
Section 10 — Lowest Safe Water Quality
Potable water shall be reserved primarily for uses requiring potable quality.
Every new building, community, industrial project, public facility, and infrastructure program shall use the lowest safe water quality appropriate to the intended purpose.
The Authority shall establish national water-quality classifications, including:

items: Potable Water Water safe for drinking, food preparation, and other direct human consumption.

textBlocks.text: Sanitary Water Water suitable for personal washing, sanitation, and other human-contact uses not requiring drinking-water quality.
Utility Water Water suitable for cleaning, flushing, cooling, fire protection, and general building operations.
Agricultural Water Water suitable for irrigation, livestock, or food production according to crop and exposure standards.
Industrial Process Water Water treated according to the requirements of a defined industrial process.
Ecological Restoration Water Water suitable for wetlands, stream restoration, groundwater recharge, or habitat support.
Additional classifications may be established where scientifically justified.
Section 11 — Human Waste as a Recoverable Resource
Human excreta and compatible organic materials shall be classified as recoverable biological resources rather than ordinary disposable waste.
Sanitation systems shall be designed to safely recover, where technically and environmentally justified:
Recovery shall never take priority over pathogen destruction or contaminant control.
No recovered material shall enter agriculture, commerce, public land, or the environment unless it satisfies the applicable national safety standard.
Section 12 — Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities
Conventional sewage treatment plants shall progressively be replaced, converted, or integrated into Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities.
These facilities shall be designed to:

items: return useful outputs to agriculture, industry, energy systems, ecological restoration, or public use.

textBlocks.text: A facility shall not be judged successful merely because discharged water meets minimum pollution standards.

Performance shall also be evaluated according to water recovery, nutrient recovery, energy balance, contaminant removal, residual waste, operating reliability, public cost, and local environmental restoration.
Section 13 — Distributed Sanitation Infrastructure
The national system shall use distributed, building-scale, neighborhood-scale, municipal, regional, and centralized sanitation according to actual need.
No community shall be required to depend upon an enormous centralized sewer network where a smaller and more resilient system can safely provide equivalent or superior service.
Distributed systems may include:
System scale shall be selected according to public health, density, geography, cost, workforce, transport distance, resilience, maintenance capability, and expected population growth.
Section 14 — Centralization and Regional Systems
Centralized or regional sanitation infrastructure may remain appropriate for dense cities, industrial corridors, major institutions, and areas where local processing would be inefficient or unsafe.
Central facilities shall not be prohibited solely because they are large.
However, no centralized system shall be approved or retained without evaluating:

items: contamination caused by mixed waste streams.
vulnerability to cyberattack or grid failure.

textBlocks.text: Section 15 — Industrial and Specialized Wastewater

Industrial facilities, hospitals, laboratories, pharmaceutical manufacturers, chemical processors, research facilities, and other specialized operations shall separate and treat hazardous or persistent waste streams before discharge into public sanitation infrastructure.
Specialized facilities shall not transfer to the public the full cost of treating contaminants uniquely created by their operations.
Pretreatment requirements may include:
Public sanitation systems may refuse discharge that cannot be safely treated.
Section 16 — Contaminant Responsibility Standard

A manufacturer or commercial organization introducing persistent, toxic, bioactive, or difficult-to-remove contaminants into commerce shall remain responsible for the foreseeable sanitation burden created after use.
Covered contaminants may include:

items: and other materials designated by scientific review.

textBlocks.text: The responsible organization shall fund or provide technically viable systems for detection, separation, recovery, neutralization, destruction, or secure containment.
Section 17 — Product and Chemical Authorization
Where a new commercial chemical or material is likely to enter public water or sanitation systems, the producer shall submit a sanitation-impact assessment before widespread sale.
The assessment shall identify:
A product shall not receive unrestricted approval where no safe and practical management pathway exists and the expected public harm is substantial.
Section 18 — Agricultural Use of Recovered Materials
Recovered nutrients or organic materials may enter agriculture only after strict verification.
No sanitation-derived product shall be applied to crops, soil, animal feed systems, or agricultural land where concentrations of the following exceed national standards:
Standards shall account for accumulation over time, soil health, groundwater, food-chain transfer, worker exposure, and long-term ecological effects.
Section 19 — Pathogen Destruction
Every sanitation recovery process shall include verified pathogen destruction appropriate to the intended output.
Standards shall address:
No recovered water, nutrient product, organic material, fertilizer, soil amendment, or reusable component shall be released before required treatment and testing.
The Authority shall maintain independent testing and certification protocols.
Section 20 — Reusable Water Standards
Recovered water shall be assigned to the appropriate national water-quality class.
Treatment and monitoring shall match the intended use.
The intentional use of potable-quality treatment for low-risk non-potable purposes shall be discouraged where it creates unnecessary energy, chemical, or financial cost.
Conversely, water shall not be used for a higher-risk purpose than its verified quality permits.
Systems shall use clear physical, technical, and labeling safeguards to prevent accidental cross-connection.
Section 21 — Heat Recovery
Wastewater and greywater heat shall be treated as recoverable energy.
New large buildings, district systems, hospitals, industrial facilities, housing developments, laundries, and sanitation plants shall evaluate heat-recovery potential.
Where lifecycle benefit is substantial, systems shall recover heat for:
Heat-recovery requirements shall account for maintenance, fouling, contamination, reliability, and actual energy savings.
Section 22 — Energy Production
Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities shall evaluate anaerobic digestion, biogas recovery, thermal recovery, and other energy-producing methods.
Energy production shall be used to reduce the operating burden of sanitation infrastructure and improve emergency independence.
No facility shall claim energy self-sufficiency by excluding major energy inputs, chemical production, transport, or residual treatment from its accounting.
Energy performance shall be measured over the complete lifecycle.
Section 23 — Continuity During Infrastructure Failure
Sanitation systems shall remain operable during:
Systems shall maintain degraded-operation capability sufficient to prevent uncontrolled waste accumulation, sewage release, widespread contamination, or public-health collapse.
Section 24 — Emergency Operating Capability
Critical sanitation systems shall maintain:

items: gravity or passive operating modes where possible;

textBlocks.text: No essential sanitation system shall depend entirely upon cloud services, remote licensing, satellite navigation, internet access, or a single electronic control system.
Section 25 — Survival Mode Integration
Every Continuity Cell established under the national continuity system shall maintain a Sanitation Survival Plan.
The plan shall identify:
Sanitation shall receive the same priority as drinking water during Survival Mode.
Section 26 — Domestic Manufacturing Capacity
The Nation shall maintain domestic capability to manufacture, repair, and replace critical sanitation equipment.
Domestic capability shall include:
International trade may supplement domestic production.
It shall not create permanent dependence upon one foreign supplier for essential sanitation capability.
Section 27 — Open Standards and Repairability
Sanitation infrastructure shall use open and interoperable technical standards wherever reasonably practical.
Equipment shall be:

items: and capable of offline and manual operation.

textBlocks.text: Critical infrastructure shall not be designed around unnecessary proprietary restrictions.
A supplier shall not disable sanitation equipment remotely because of contract expiration, licensing disputes, or refusal to purchase unrelated services.
Section 28 — Supplier Independence
No municipality or utility shall become permanently dependent upon a single supplier where technically reasonable alternatives can be maintained.
Procurement shall consider:
Proprietary systems may be approved only where their performance advantage substantially outweighs dependency risk.
Section 29 — National Sanitation Technology Challenge Program
The Agency for the Advancement of Technology shall establish a National Sanitation Technology Challenge Program in coordination with the Authority.

The Program shall invite universities, engineers, manufacturers, students, municipalities, public laboratories, research institutions, cooperatives, and other qualified participants to develop improved sanitation systems.
Challenge areas may include:
Section 30 — Technology Evaluation
Sanitation technologies shall be evaluated according to:
No technology shall receive national adoption solely because it performs well in laboratory conditions.
Field reliability, maintenance burden, workforce requirements, behavior under misuse, and emergency operation shall be evaluated.
Section 31 — Pilot Communities
The Authority may designate pilot communities for testing advanced sanitation systems.
Participation shall require:

items: restoration plans if the pilot fails.

textBlocks.text: Pilot status shall not reduce residents’ rights to safe sanitation.
Residents shall not be forced to remain in unsafe or materially inferior conditions for the sake of experimentation.
Section 32 — Pilot Transparency
Every pilot shall publish a Pilot Sanitation Record stating:
Commercial secrecy shall not conceal public-health or performance failures.
Section 33 — Termination of Unsuccessful Systems
A pilot or deployed sanitation technology shall be suspended, redesigned, or terminated where it creates:

items: lower resilience than the replaced system;
or serious public dissatisfaction unsupported by corrective action.

textBlocks.text: Termination shall include restoration of safe sanitation service.
Section 34 — Existing Sewer Infrastructure
Existing sewer systems shall remain safely maintained throughout the transition.
Municipalities and utilities shall not defer necessary repair merely because long-term replacement is planned.
Existing systems shall progressively:
Section 35 — Combined Sewer Overflows
Combined sewer overflows shall be treated as preventable infrastructure failures.
States and municipalities shall establish binding schedules to separate stormwater and sanitary flows or otherwise prevent untreated discharge.
Temporary overflow events shall be publicly reported.
Repeated overflow without corrective action shall result in enforcement and mandatory infrastructure intervention.
No locality shall indefinitely rely upon rainfall as a justification for releasing untreated sewage.
Section 36 — Retirement of Conventional Infrastructure
A conventional sewer segment or treatment facility may be retired only after replacement capacity is:
Retirement plans shall include land restoration, material recovery, pipe reuse where appropriate, contamination assessment, and safe disposition of abandoned infrastructure.
Section 37 — Transition Duration
The national transition shall occur over several decades.
The Authority shall establish a national transition period not exceeding fifty years, subject to earlier completion where practical.
The transition shall proceed through binding phases.

Phase I — Measurement and Preparation map existing sanitation systems; measure water used for waste transport; identify contamination sources; establish national standards; create manufacturing and workforce plans; and begin pilot programs.
Phase II — New Construction Conversion require low-water and source-separating systems in designated new construction; establish greywater reuse; and prevent new combined sewer infrastructure.
Phase III — Municipal and Industrial Modernization convert major facilities; separate hazardous waste streams; establish neighborhood systems; and expand resource recovery.
Phase IV — Existing Building Conversion retrofit buildings during major renovation; retire obsolete sewer districts; and expand distributed sanitation.

Phase V — National Completion prohibit routine potable-water waste transport where alternatives exist; prohibit routine discharge of recoverable sanitation resources; and complete replacement of conventional infrastructure.
Section 38 — Federal Facilities
National government buildings, military facilities, research institutions, hospitals, prisons, transportation hubs, and other federal properties shall lead the transition.
New federal construction shall adopt approved circular sanitation systems according to accelerated deadlines.
Existing federal facilities shall complete sanitation transition plans and serve as demonstration sites where appropriate.
Classified or defense facilities shall remain subject to health and environmental standards even where operational details cannot be public.
Section 39 — State and Municipal Milestones
Every state and municipality shall adopt a Circular Sanitation Transition Plan.
The plan shall identify:
Plans shall be updated at least every five years.
Section 40 — Industrial Milestones
Industrial and specialized facilities shall meet accelerated separation and pretreatment deadlines.
No industrial facility shall delay contaminant control on the ground that the municipal system will eventually be replaced.
Where an industry creates a contaminant burden requiring new public treatment infrastructure, the responsible industry shall finance the attributable cost.
Section 41 — Treatment-Plant Milestones
Every existing sewage treatment plant shall receive a transition classification.
Classifications may include:
Each plant shall have binding targets for water recovery, nutrient recovery, energy recovery, contaminant removal, overflow prevention, and residual reduction.
Section 42 — Eventual Potable-Water Restriction
After approved alternatives are reasonably available, the routine use of potable water primarily to transport human waste shall be prohibited.
Exceptions may be permitted where:

items: The alternative would create greater health risk.
conversion cost is grossly disproportionate to public benefit.
the use is incidental and unavoidable within an otherwise compliant system.

textBlocks.text: Exceptions shall be reviewed periodically and shall not become permanent without evidence.
Section 43 — Eventual Discharge Prohibition
The routine discharge of the following into rivers, lakes, groundwater, oceans, wetlands, or the environment shall eventually be prohibited:

items: persistent chemicals that can reasonably be removed or destroyed.

textBlocks.text: Discharge may remain lawful only where treatment, recovery, environmental impact, and ecological need justify it.
Section 44 — Residual Materials
Materials that cannot be safely reused, recovered, neutralized, or destroyed shall be placed in secure, monitored containment.
Residuals shall remain inventoried.
Containment systems shall:
The creation of a residual stream shall not relieve responsible producers or utilities of continuing management obligations.
Section 45 — National Sanitation Materials Database
The Authority shall maintain a National Sanitation Materials Database.
The Database shall track, at appropriate aggregate or facility level:
The system shall not require unnecessary collection of individually identifiable personal information.
Section 46 — Sanitation Privacy
Sanitation monitoring shall not be used to create personal medical or behavioral profiles.

No sanitation authority, utility, contractor, research organization, employer, insurer, law-enforcement body, or other institution shall use sanitation-derived information to identify an individual’s:
Building-level monitoring shall use the least identifying data reasonably necessary.
Section 47 — Public-Health Emergency Exception
Narrowly limited analysis may occur where credible evidence identifies an immediate and specific public-health threat.
Such analysis shall require:

items: approval by an authorized public-health official.

textBlocks.text: The exception shall not authorize general surveillance or routine law-enforcement access.
Section 48 — Public Ownership and Utility Operation
Essential sanitation infrastructure shall be publicly owned, cooperatively owned, nonprofit operated, or operated as a tightly regulated utility.
Private operation may be permitted where performance, affordability, maintenance, transparency, and continuity standards are satisfied.
No operator shall extract excessive profit from essential sanitation services.
Revenue shall be used primarily for:
Section 49 — Universal Affordability
No household shall be denied safe sanitation because of inability to pay.
Sanitation charges shall be affordable relative to household income.
Low-income households, medically vulnerable residents, disabled persons, and persons experiencing hardship shall receive reduced charges or public support.
Nonpayment alone shall not justify termination of essential sanitation service where doing so would create unsafe or unsanitary conditions.
Fraud and deliberate refusal to comply with reasonable payment arrangements may receive separate enforcement.
Section 50 — Public Information
Residents shall receive clear information regarding:
Public education shall avoid technical obscurity and shall not misrepresent experimental technology as proven.
Section 51 — Citizen Complaints
Every sanitation operator and government authority shall maintain accessible complaint procedures.
Residents may report:
Complaints shall be tracked, investigated, and resolved within defined periods.
Retaliation against a complainant shall be prohibited.
Section 52 — Independent Scientific Review
Major sanitation standards, contaminant limits, recovery methods, reuse classifications, and technology approvals shall undergo independent scientific review.
Review panels shall include expertise in:
Commercial participants shall disclose conflicts of interest.
Section 53 — National Action Records

Every major sanitation program, infrastructure project, technological mandate, contaminant standard, transition milestone, facility conversion, or reuse rule shall possess a concise National Action Record.
The Record shall state:
Technical annexes may contain detailed engineering information, but the public record shall remain concise and understandable.
Section 54 — Independent Performance Measurement
The Office of National Performance and Accountability shall independently measure:

items: progress toward elimination of conventional water-carried sewage.

textBlocks.text: Methods, assumptions, confidence ranges, and limitations shall be public.
Section 55 — Enforcement
Violations may result in:

items: executive compensation clawbacks where misconduct was knowingly directed.
criminal prosecution for deliberate contamination, falsification, concealment, or evasion.

textBlocks.text: Penalties shall account for harm, duration, knowledge, volume, concealment, public cost, and compliance history.
Section 56 — Deliberate Contamination and Evasion
No person or organization shall:

items: dilute waste to avoid concentration limits.
export sanitation residuals for unsafe disposal.
create shell entities to avoid responsibility.

textBlocks.text: Deliberate conduct creating substantial risk to public health or water resources shall constitute an aggravated offense.
Section 57 — Due Process
No facility, product, technology, utility, or property shall be restricted without notice, explanation, access to evidence, opportunity to respond, and meaningful appeal.
Emergency suspension may occur where immediate danger exists.
Emergency action shall receive rapid independent review.
Technical disputes shall be heard by qualified reviewers.
Final compulsory orders, major penalties, public acquisition, or permanent operating prohibitions shall remain subject to judicial review.
Section 58 — Judicial Review
Courts may review:
Courts shall give appropriate weight to independent scientific evidence while preserving constitutional rights and procedural fairness.
Section 59 — Workforce Development
The Nation shall establish education, apprenticeship, and certification programs for the circular sanitation workforce.
Training shall include:
Existing wastewater workers shall receive priority access to retraining and transition employment.
Section 60 — Financing
Sanitation modernization may be funded through:
Private financing shall not purchase regulatory control, ownership of public sanitation data, or unrestricted profit rights over essential services.
Section 61 — Small Communities and Rural Areas
Rural communities, remote settlements, farms, tribal communities, and low-density regions shall receive sanitation solutions appropriate to their conditions.
The Nation shall support:
Low population shall not justify denial of safe sanitation.
Rural systems shall not be forced into expensive centralized sewers where decentralized systems are safer and more practical.
Section 62 — Dense Urban Areas
Dense cities shall receive coordinated district-scale and building-scale transition plans.
Urban programs shall address:
Urban conversion shall proceed district by district to prevent citywide disruption.
Section 63 — Housing Integration
National housing programs shall incorporate circular sanitation requirements.

New lowest-tier housing, converted commercial buildings, nonprofit rentals, community land trusts, and first-time ownership projects shall use approved low-water or source-separating systems according to applicable transition phases.
Sanitation modernization shall not be used to make affordable housing financially inaccessible.
Public support shall cover additional capital cost where required.
Section 64 — Material Circulation Integration
Sanitation-derived materials shall enter the National Material Circulation Database where appropriate.
Recovered nutrients, metals, plastics, chemicals, organic material, and residuals shall receive standardized classifications.
Sanitation infrastructure shall comply with product repairability, material identification, component recovery, and landfill-elimination requirements.
Section 65 — Environmental Restoration

The transition shall restore rivers, lakes, wetlands, groundwater, coastlines, and marine environments damaged by sewage discharge, nutrient loading, combined sewer overflows, chemical contamination, and wastewater infrastructure.
Restoration programs shall measure:
Polluters responsible for identifiable damage may be required to fund restoration.
Section 66 — National Review
Every five years, the Authority shall publish a National Circular Sanitation Review.
The public report shall identify:
The report shall remain concise, with technical annexes maintained separately.
Section 67 — Final Constitutional Standard

The Nation shall complete the transition from a sanitation system that dilutes valuable biological materials with clean water and discharges them as waste to a sanitation system that safely separates, concentrates, treats, recovers, reuses, and circulates water, nutrients, energy, heat, organic material, and other useful resources.
The completed system shall preserve health, dignity, affordability, privacy, resilience, ecological integrity, and technological independence.
Clean water shall no longer be routinely consumed merely to move human waste from one location to another.

Performance shall also be evaluated according to water recovery, nutrient recovery, energy balance, contaminant removal, residual waste, operating reliability, public cost, and local environmental restoration.
Section 13 — Distributed Sanitation Infrastructure
The national system shall use distributed, building-scale, neighborhood-scale, municipal, regional, and centralized sanitation according to actual need.
No community shall be required to depend upon an enormous centralized sewer network where a smaller and more resilient system can safely provide equivalent or superior service.
Distributed systems may include:

building-level treatment; block or neighborhood collection; local vacuum networks; community digesters; modular recovery facilities; shared greywater systems; mobile sanitation units; and regional processing centers.
System scale shall be selected according to public health, density, geography, cost, workforce, transport distance, resilience, maintenance capability, and expected population growth.
Section 14 — Centralization and Regional Systems
Centralized or regional sanitation infrastructure may remain appropriate for dense cities, industrial corridors, major institutions, and areas where local processing would be inefficient or unsafe.
Central facilities shall not be prohibited solely because they are large.
However, no centralized system shall be approved or retained without evaluating:

Single points of failure. Sewer-network leakage. Pumping energy. overflow risk. contamination caused by mixed waste streams. restoration difficulty. vulnerability to cyberattack or grid failure. availability of distributed alternatives. resource-recovery performance. long-term replacement cost.
Section 15 — Industrial and Specialized Wastewater

Industrial facilities, hospitals, laboratories, pharmaceutical manufacturers, chemical processors, research facilities, and other specialized operations shall separate and treat hazardous or persistent waste streams before discharge into public sanitation infrastructure.
Specialized facilities shall not transfer to the public the full cost of treating contaminants uniquely created by their operations.
Pretreatment requirements may include:

Chemical separation. Drug and pharmaceutical recovery. Pathogen destruction. Radioactive-material control. heavy-metal removal. PFAS treatment. microplastic capture. solvent recovery. neutralization. secure residual containment.
Public sanitation systems may refuse discharge that cannot be safely treated.
Section 16 — Contaminant Responsibility Standard

A manufacturer or commercial organization introducing persistent, toxic, bioactive, or difficult-to-remove contaminants into commerce shall remain responsible for the foreseeable sanitation burden created after use.
Covered contaminants may include:

PFAS; pharmaceuticals; endocrine-disrupting chemicals; microplastics; nanomaterials; heavy metals; persistent solvents; antimicrobial compounds; industrial additives; and other materials designated by scientific review.
The responsible organization shall fund or provide technically viable systems for detection, separation, recovery, neutralization, destruction, or secure containment.
Section 17 — Product and Chemical Authorization
Where a new commercial chemical or material is likely to enter public water or sanitation systems, the producer shall submit a sanitation-impact assessment before widespread sale.
The assessment shall identify:

Expected pathways into wastewater. Persistence. Toxicity. Bioaccumulation. Treatment requirements. Detection methods. Expected concentration. Recovery or destruction methods. Long-term containment needs. Cost to public sanitation systems.
A product shall not receive unrestricted approval where no safe and practical management pathway exists and the expected public harm is substantial.
Section 18 — Agricultural Use of Recovered Materials
Recovered nutrients or organic materials may enter agriculture only after strict verification.
No sanitation-derived product shall be applied to crops, soil, animal feed systems, or agricultural land where concentrations of the following exceed national standards:
Pathogens. Pharmaceuticals. PFAS. Heavy metals. Microplastics. Hormone-active compounds. Toxic industrial chemicals. Persistent biological contaminants. Other dangerous materials.
Standards shall account for accumulation over time, soil health, groundwater, food-chain transfer, worker exposure, and long-term ecological effects.
Section 19 — Pathogen Destruction
Every sanitation recovery process shall include verified pathogen destruction appropriate to the intended output.
Standards shall address:
bacteria; viruses; parasites; spores; antibiotic-resistant organisms; and other disease-causing agents.
No recovered water, nutrient product, organic material, fertilizer, soil amendment, or reusable component shall be released before required treatment and testing.
The Authority shall maintain independent testing and certification protocols.
Section 20 — Reusable Water Standards
Recovered water shall be assigned to the appropriate national water-quality class.
Treatment and monitoring shall match the intended use.
The intentional use of potable-quality treatment for low-risk non-potable purposes shall be discouraged where it creates unnecessary energy, chemical, or financial cost.
Conversely, water shall not be used for a higher-risk purpose than its verified quality permits.
Systems shall use clear physical, technical, and labeling safeguards to prevent accidental cross-connection.
Section 21 — Heat Recovery
Wastewater and greywater heat shall be treated as recoverable energy.
New large buildings, district systems, hospitals, industrial facilities, housing developments, laundries, and sanitation plants shall evaluate heat-recovery potential.
Where lifecycle benefit is substantial, systems shall recover heat for:
Water heating. Space heating. District energy. Industrial processes. Thermal storage. Other useful purposes.
Heat-recovery requirements shall account for maintenance, fouling, contamination, reliability, and actual energy savings.
Section 22 — Energy Production
Resource Recovery and Sanitation Facilities shall evaluate anaerobic digestion, biogas recovery, thermal recovery, and other energy-producing methods.
Energy production shall be used to reduce the operating burden of sanitation infrastructure and improve emergency independence.
No facility shall claim energy self-sufficiency by excluding major energy inputs, chemical production, transport, or residual treatment from its accounting.
Energy performance shall be measured over the complete lifecycle.
Section 23 — Continuity During Infrastructure Failure
Sanitation systems shall remain operable during:
Electrical-grid failure. Internet failure. Fuel shortage. Natural disaster. War. Cyberattack. Supply-chain disruption. Regional isolation. National Survival Mode. Other prolonged emergencies.
Systems shall maintain degraded-operation capability sufficient to prevent uncontrolled waste accumulation, sewage release, widespread contamination, or public-health collapse.
Section 24 — Emergency Operating Capability
Critical sanitation systems shall maintain:

backup power; gravity or passive operating modes where possible; emergency storage; offline controls; manual operation procedures; local authentication; reserve sanitation chemicals; replacement pumps and valves; portable treatment systems; mobile toilets and collection equipment; emergency maintenance crews; and independent communications.
No essential sanitation system shall depend entirely upon cloud services, remote licensing, satellite navigation, internet access, or a single electronic control system.
Section 25 — Survival Mode Integration
Every Continuity Cell established under the national continuity system shall maintain a Sanitation Survival Plan.
The plan shall identify:

Minimum sanitary capacity. Emergency collection methods. Temporary waterless systems. Mobile sanitation facilities. Waste-storage limits. pathogen-control methods. vulnerable populations. emergency staffing. fuel and energy requirements. restoration priorities.
Sanitation shall receive the same priority as drinking water during Survival Mode.
Section 26 — Domestic Manufacturing Capacity
The Nation shall maintain domestic capability to manufacture, repair, and replace critical sanitation equipment.
Domestic capability shall include:

Pumps. Vacuum systems. Valves. pipes and fittings. storage vessels. treatment systems. membranes. sensors. digesters. separation equipment. heat exchangers. recovery systems. sanitation chemicals. control systems. mobile sanitation equipment. critical replacement components.
International trade may supplement domestic production.
It shall not create permanent dependence upon one foreign supplier for essential sanitation capability.
Section 27 — Open Standards and Repairability
Sanitation infrastructure shall use open and interoperable technical standards wherever reasonably practical.
Equipment shall be:
repairable; modular; accessible for maintenance; compatible with interchangeable parts; supported for long service lives; and capable of offline and manual operation.
Critical infrastructure shall not be designed around unnecessary proprietary restrictions.
A supplier shall not disable sanitation equipment remotely because of contract expiration, licensing disputes, or refusal to purchase unrelated services.
Section 28 — Supplier Independence
No municipality or utility shall become permanently dependent upon a single supplier where technically reasonable alternatives can be maintained.
Procurement shall consider:

Component interchangeability. Availability of repair information. Domestic replacement capability. Open control systems. software continuity. cybersecurity. training availability. long-term support. total lifecycle cost. emergency substitution.
Proprietary systems may be approved only where their performance advantage substantially outweighs dependency risk.
Section 29 — National Sanitation Technology Challenge Program
The Agency for the Advancement of Technology shall establish a National Sanitation Technology Challenge Program in coordination with the Authority.

The Program shall invite universities, engineers, manufacturers, students, municipalities, public laboratories, research institutions, cooperatives, and other qualified participants to develop improved sanitation systems.
Challenge areas may include:

Waterless toilets. Vacuum sanitation. Source separation. Pathogen destruction. nutrient recovery. pharmaceutical removal. PFAS destruction. microplastic capture. compact neighborhood treatment. low-energy desalination of wastewater. greywater reuse. heat recovery. sanitation during emergencies. low-maintenance rural systems. accessible sanitation. odor and user-experience improvement.
Section 30 — Technology Evaluation
Sanitation technologies shall be evaluated according to:

water conservation; public-health safety; pathogen destruction; contaminant control; resource recovery; energy consumption; repairability; resilience; domestic manufacturability; lifecycle cost; environmental effects; scalability; user dignity; public acceptance; and quality of life.
No technology shall receive national adoption solely because it performs well in laboratory conditions.
Field reliability, maintenance burden, workforce requirements, behavior under misuse, and emergency operation shall be evaluated.
Section 31 — Pilot Communities
The Authority may designate pilot communities for testing advanced sanitation systems.
Participation shall require:

Public notice. Resident consultation. independent scientific review. verified backup sanitation. health monitoring. environmental monitoring. transparent cost reporting. complaint procedures. termination criteria. restoration plans if the pilot fails.
Pilot status shall not reduce residents’ rights to safe sanitation.
Residents shall not be forced to remain in unsafe or materially inferior conditions for the sake of experimentation.
Section 32 — Pilot Transparency
Every pilot shall publish a Pilot Sanitation Record stating:

Technology tested. Population served. Expected water savings. Expected recovery outputs. Known risks. Cost. Maintenance requirements. monitoring methods. health findings. user complaints. system failures. final recommendation.
Commercial secrecy shall not conceal public-health or performance failures.
Section 33 — Termination of Unsuccessful Systems
A pilot or deployed sanitation technology shall be suspended, redesigned, or terminated where it creates:

unsafe exposure; repeated system failure; persistent odor or habitability problems; unreasonable maintenance burden; excessive cost; inadequate pathogen destruction; contaminant release; lower resilience than the replaced system; or serious public dissatisfaction unsupported by corrective action.
Termination shall include restoration of safe sanitation service.
Section 34 — Existing Sewer Infrastructure
Existing sewer systems shall remain safely maintained throughout the transition.
Municipalities and utilities shall not defer necessary repair merely because long-term replacement is planned.
Existing systems shall progressively:

Reduce leakage. Reduce potable-water demand. eliminate combined sewer overflows. separate stormwater. separate industrial waste. increase water recovery. increase nutrient recovery. recover heat and energy. improve cyber and grid resilience. prepare sections for retirement.
Section 35 — Combined Sewer Overflows
Combined sewer overflows shall be treated as preventable infrastructure failures.
States and municipalities shall establish binding schedules to separate stormwater and sanitary flows or otherwise prevent untreated discharge.
Temporary overflow events shall be publicly reported.
Repeated overflow without corrective action shall result in enforcement and mandatory infrastructure intervention.
No locality shall indefinitely rely upon rainfall as a justification for releasing untreated sewage.
Section 36 — Retirement of Conventional Infrastructure
A conventional sewer segment or treatment facility may be retired only after replacement capacity is:
Constructed. Tested. Certified. Staffed. supplied with replacement components. integrated into emergency plans. accepted for operation. demonstrated to protect public health.
Retirement plans shall include land restoration, material recovery, pipe reuse where appropriate, contamination assessment, and safe disposition of abandoned infrastructure.
Section 37 — Transition Duration
The national transition shall occur over several decades.
The Authority shall establish a national transition period not exceeding fifty years, subject to earlier completion where practical.
The transition shall proceed through binding phases.

Phase I — Measurement and Preparation map existing sanitation systems; measure water used for waste transport; identify contamination sources; establish national standards; create manufacturing and workforce plans; and begin pilot programs.
Phase II — New Construction Conversion require low-water and source-separating systems in designated new construction; establish greywater reuse; and prevent new combined sewer infrastructure.
Phase III — Municipal and Industrial Modernization convert major facilities; separate hazardous waste streams; establish neighborhood systems; and expand resource recovery.
Phase IV — Existing Building Conversion retrofit buildings during major renovation; retire obsolete sewer districts; and expand distributed sanitation.

Phase V — National Completion prohibit routine potable-water waste transport where alternatives exist; prohibit routine discharge of recoverable sanitation resources; and complete replacement of conventional infrastructure.
Section 38 — Federal Facilities
National government buildings, military facilities, research institutions, hospitals, prisons, transportation hubs, and other federal properties shall lead the transition.
New federal construction shall adopt approved circular sanitation systems according to accelerated deadlines.
Existing federal facilities shall complete sanitation transition plans and serve as demonstration sites where appropriate.
Classified or defense facilities shall remain subject to health and environmental standards even where operational details cannot be public.
Section 39 — State and Municipal Milestones
Every state and municipality shall adopt a Circular Sanitation Transition Plan.
The plan shall identify:

Existing infrastructure. Potable-water use for sanitation. sewer overflow history. treatment performance. industrial contamination. greywater potential. suitable distributed systems. priority conversion districts. workforce requirements. funding requirements. public-health safeguards. milestone dates.
Plans shall be updated at least every five years.
Section 40 — Industrial Milestones
Industrial and specialized facilities shall meet accelerated separation and pretreatment deadlines.
No industrial facility shall delay contaminant control on the ground that the municipal system will eventually be replaced.
Where an industry creates a contaminant burden requiring new public treatment infrastructure, the responsible industry shall finance the attributable cost.
Section 41 — Treatment-Plant Milestones
Every existing sewage treatment plant shall receive a transition classification.
Classifications may include:

Conversion to full resource recovery. Partial conversion and regional integration. Replacement with distributed facilities. continued transitional operation. planned retirement. specialized residual treatment.
Each plant shall have binding targets for water recovery, nutrient recovery, energy recovery, contaminant removal, overflow prevention, and residual reduction.
Section 42 — Eventual Potable-Water Restriction
After approved alternatives are reasonably available, the routine use of potable water primarily to transport human waste shall be prohibited.
Exceptions may be permitted where:

The alternative would create greater health risk. geography makes alternatives impractical. temporary emergency conditions exist. conversion cost is grossly disproportionate to public benefit. the use is incidental and unavoidable within an otherwise compliant system.
Exceptions shall be reviewed periodically and shall not become permanent without evidence.
Section 43 — Eventual Discharge Prohibition
The routine discharge of the following into rivers, lakes, groundwater, oceans, wetlands, or the environment shall eventually be prohibited:

Untreated sewage. recoverable nutrients. reusable water. economically recoverable energy. preventable contaminants. untreated industrial waste. pathogen-bearing residuals. avoidable microplastics. persistent chemicals that can reasonably be removed or destroyed.
Discharge may remain lawful only where treatment, recovery, environmental impact, and ecological need justify it.
Section 44 — Residual Materials
Materials that cannot be safely reused, recovered, neutralized, or destroyed shall be placed in secure, monitored containment.
Residuals shall remain inventoried.
Containment systems shall:
prevent release; remain accessible for future treatment; separate incompatible materials; maintain chain-of-custody records; undergo periodic review; and avoid untracked burial.
The creation of a residual stream shall not relieve responsible producers or utilities of continuing management obligations.
Section 45 — National Sanitation Materials Database
The Authority shall maintain a National Sanitation Materials Database.
The Database shall track, at appropriate aggregate or facility level:

Human waste generation. Greywater generation. Industrial wastewater. contaminant loads. treatment pathways. recovered water. recovered nutrients. recovered energy. recovered materials. destroyed contaminants. residual containment. facility performance. transport between facilities. environmental discharge.
The system shall not require unnecessary collection of individually identifiable personal information.
Section 46 — Sanitation Privacy
Sanitation monitoring shall not be used to create personal medical or behavioral profiles.

No sanitation authority, utility, contractor, research organization, employer, insurer, law-enforcement body, or other institution shall use sanitation-derived information to identify an individual’s:
Medical condition. Drug use. Pregnancy. Genetic characteristics. Reproductive activity. Diet. Political activity. Religious practice. Personal relationships. Other private behavior.
Building-level monitoring shall use the least identifying data reasonably necessary.
Section 47 — Public-Health Emergency Exception
Narrowly limited analysis may occur where credible evidence identifies an immediate and specific public-health threat.
Such analysis shall require:

A defined emergency purpose. approval by an authorized public-health official. independent legal oversight. minimum necessary data. time limits. destruction or de-identification after use. prohibition of unrelated use. later review.
The exception shall not authorize general surveillance or routine law-enforcement access.
Section 48 — Public Ownership and Utility Operation
Essential sanitation infrastructure shall be publicly owned, cooperatively owned, nonprofit operated, or operated as a tightly regulated utility.
Private operation may be permitted where performance, affordability, maintenance, transparency, and continuity standards are satisfied.
No operator shall extract excessive profit from essential sanitation services.
Revenue shall be used primarily for:
operation; maintenance; workforce; modernization; reserve capacity; public-health protection; emergency readiness; and system replacement.
Section 49 — Universal Affordability
No household shall be denied safe sanitation because of inability to pay.
Sanitation charges shall be affordable relative to household income.
Low-income households, medically vulnerable residents, disabled persons, and persons experiencing hardship shall receive reduced charges or public support.
Nonpayment alone shall not justify termination of essential sanitation service where doing so would create unsafe or unsanitary conditions.
Fraud and deliberate refusal to comply with reasonable payment arrangements may receive separate enforcement.
Section 50 — Public Information
Residents shall receive clear information regarding:

The sanitation system serving them. How the system operates. What may enter the system. Maintenance responsibilities. emergency procedures. water-reuse classifications. health protections. complaint methods. service charges. transition plans.
Public education shall avoid technical obscurity and shall not misrepresent experimental technology as proven.
Section 51 — Citizen Complaints
Every sanitation operator and government authority shall maintain accessible complaint procedures.
Residents may report:
odor; leakage; contamination; service failure; unsafe reuse; privacy violations; billing abuse; neglected maintenance; illegal discharge; or other sanitation concerns.
Complaints shall be tracked, investigated, and resolved within defined periods.
Retaliation against a complainant shall be prohibited.
Section 52 — Independent Scientific Review
Major sanitation standards, contaminant limits, recovery methods, reuse classifications, and technology approvals shall undergo independent scientific review.
Review panels shall include expertise in:
Public health. microbiology. environmental engineering. water treatment. toxicology. chemistry. agriculture. energy systems. infrastructure. epidemiology. occupational safety. community health.
Commercial participants shall disclose conflicts of interest.
Section 53 — National Action Records

Every major sanitation program, infrastructure project, technological mandate, contaminant standard, transition milestone, facility conversion, or reuse rule shall possess a concise National Action Record.
The Record shall state:

Purpose. Population served. Cost. Expected water savings. Expected health effects. Expected environmental effects. Resource-recovery outputs. Energy effects. Technological requirements. Maintenance requirements. Success criteria. Failure criteria. Review date. Expiration or renewal conditions.
Technical annexes may contain detailed engineering information, but the public record shall remain concise and understandable.
Section 54 — Independent Performance Measurement
The Office of National Performance and Accountability shall independently measure:

Potable-water consumption for sanitation. Greywater recovery. Reusable-water production. sanitation-related disease. infrastructure failures. sewer overflows. contaminant release. phosphorus recovery. nitrogen recovery. energy recovery. heat recovery. material recovery. residual waste. system reliability. emergency performance. household cost. public satisfaction. domestic manufacturing. technological performance. environmental restoration. progress toward elimination of conventional water-carried sewage.
Methods, assumptions, confidence ranges, and limitations shall be public.
Section 55 — Enforcement
Violations may result in:

Corrective orders. mandatory repair. mandatory pretreatment. suspension of discharge rights. compensation for affected residents. environmental restoration. civil penalties. increased contaminant responsibility payments. executive compensation clawbacks where misconduct was knowingly directed. independent operational control. corporate probation. public blacklisting. revocation of operating authority. criminal prosecution for deliberate contamination, falsification, concealment, or evasion.
Penalties shall account for harm, duration, knowledge, volume, concealment, public cost, and compliance history.
Section 56 — Deliberate Contamination and Evasion
No person or organization shall:

Conceal contaminant releases. bypass required treatment. dilute waste to avoid concentration limits. misclassify hazardous discharge. falsify recovery data. interfere with monitoring. export sanitation residuals for unsafe disposal. disable safety systems. operate unauthorized discharge connections. create shell entities to avoid responsibility.
Deliberate conduct creating substantial risk to public health or water resources shall constitute an aggravated offense.
Section 57 — Due Process
No facility, product, technology, utility, or property shall be restricted without notice, explanation, access to evidence, opportunity to respond, and meaningful appeal.
Emergency suspension may occur where immediate danger exists.
Emergency action shall receive rapid independent review.
Technical disputes shall be heard by qualified reviewers.
Final compulsory orders, major penalties, public acquisition, or permanent operating prohibitions shall remain subject to judicial review.
Section 58 — Judicial Review
Courts may review:

Technology approvals. contaminant classifications. reuse restrictions. utility rates. privacy violations. emergency actions. compulsory infrastructure orders. penalties. public acquisition. transition deadlines.
Courts shall give appropriate weight to independent scientific evidence while preserving constitutional rights and procedural fairness.
Section 59 — Workforce Development
The Nation shall establish education, apprenticeship, and certification programs for the circular sanitation workforce.
Training shall include:

plumbing; vacuum systems; water treatment; biological treatment; laboratory testing; environmental engineering; microbiology; maintenance; controls; cybersecurity; resource recovery; emergency sanitation; and public-health operation.
Existing wastewater workers shall receive priority access to retraining and transition employment.
Section 60 — Financing
Sanitation modernization may be funded through:

National infrastructure appropriations. State and municipal contributions. regulated utility revenue. public infrastructure bonds. contaminant responsibility payments. industrial pretreatment charges. recovered-resource revenue. energy savings. water savings. other lawful public revenue.
Private financing shall not purchase regulatory control, ownership of public sanitation data, or unrestricted profit rights over essential services.
Section 61 — Small Communities and Rural Areas
Rural communities, remote settlements, farms, tribal communities, and low-density regions shall receive sanitation solutions appropriate to their conditions.
The Nation shall support:
modular treatment; waterless systems; local digestion; sealed collection; mobile servicing; small-scale greywater reuse; and regional shared facilities.
Low population shall not justify denial of safe sanitation.
Rural systems shall not be forced into expensive centralized sewers where decentralized systems are safer and more practical.
Section 62 — Dense Urban Areas
Dense cities shall receive coordinated district-scale and building-scale transition plans.
Urban programs shall address:

Limited space. high-rise plumbing. underground infrastructure. population concentration. commercial activity. transit facilities. hospitals and institutions. emergency evacuation. odor control. continuous service during conversion.
Urban conversion shall proceed district by district to prevent citywide disruption.
Section 63 — Housing Integration
National housing programs shall incorporate circular sanitation requirements.

New lowest-tier housing, converted commercial buildings, nonprofit rentals, community land trusts, and first-time ownership projects shall use approved low-water or source-separating systems according to applicable transition phases.
Sanitation modernization shall not be used to make affordable housing financially inaccessible.
Public support shall cover additional capital cost where required.
Section 64 — Material Circulation Integration
Sanitation-derived materials shall enter the National Material Circulation Database where appropriate.
Recovered nutrients, metals, plastics, chemicals, organic material, and residuals shall receive standardized classifications.
Sanitation infrastructure shall comply with product repairability, material identification, component recovery, and landfill-elimination requirements.
Section 65 — Environmental Restoration

The transition shall restore rivers, lakes, wetlands, groundwater, coastlines, and marine environments damaged by sewage discharge, nutrient loading, combined sewer overflows, chemical contamination, and wastewater infrastructure.
Restoration programs shall measure:
Pathogen reduction. oxygen recovery. algal-bloom reduction. habitat improvement. groundwater quality. public access. fish and wildlife recovery. long-term ecological stability.
Polluters responsible for identifiable damage may be required to fund restoration.
Section 66 — National Review
Every five years, the Authority shall publish a National Circular Sanitation Review.
The public report shall identify:

Water conserved. Population served by advanced systems. systems converted. conventional systems retired. contaminant trends. health outcomes. recovery outputs. system failures. cost. public satisfaction. manufacturing readiness. emergency readiness. missed milestones. corrective actions. remaining barriers.
The report shall remain concise, with technical annexes maintained separately.
Section 67 — Final Constitutional Standard

The Nation shall complete the transition from a sanitation system that dilutes valuable biological materials with clean water and discharges them as waste to a sanitation system that safely separates, concentrates, treats, recovers, reuses, and circulates water, nutrients, energy, heat, organic material, and other useful resources.
The completed system shall preserve health, dignity, affordability, privacy, resilience, ecological integrity, and technological independence.
Clean water shall no longer be routinely consumed merely to move human waste from one location to another.

articles.title: Consumer Services, Continuing Payments, Digital Property, and Service Continuity
articles.summary: Consumer services shall remain profitable because consumers continue to choose them and providers continue to deliver promised value. No business shall obtain continuing revenue primarily through confusion, artificial dependency, involuntary renewal, service regression, hidden charges, revocable digital ownership, or destruction of purchased value.

excerpt: Consumer services shall remain profitable because consumers continue to choose them and providers continue to deliver promised value. No business shall obtain continuing revenue primarily through confusion, artificial dependency, involuntary renewal, service regression, hidden charges, revocable digital ownership, or destruction of purchased value.
Section 1 — Scope and Exclusions

This Article governs subscriptions, memberships, automatic renewals, digital platforms, software and cloud services, streaming, telecommunications, internet access, gaming, data storage, AI services, monitoring, maintenance plans, repair plans, support programs, delivery memberships, fitness and recreation memberships, education services, transportation memberships, connected-vehicle services, recurring deliveries, and similar continuing or access-based consumer arrangements. It does not govern rent, mortgages, lending, credit agreements, insurance, taxes, government fees, court-ordered payments, or ordinary installment purchases resulting in ownership without a continuing service.
Section 2 — Consumer Service Record and Dashboard

Every covered service shall maintain a standardized record stating provider, service, price, billing frequency, enrollment, renewal, benefits, limitations, advertising, data practices, cancellation, refunds, change history, and consent history. An interoperable national dashboard shall allow consumers to view active services, monthly and annualized cost, billing and renewal dates, trials, changes, and cancellation methods without permitting government sale or commercial profiling of preferences.
Section 3 — Price Continuity and Non-Regression

textBlocks.text: Consumer services shall remain profitable because consumers continue to choose them and providers continue to deliver promised value. No business shall obtain continuing revenue primarily through confusion, artificial dependency, involuntary renewal, service regression, hidden charges, revocable digital ownership, or destruction of purchased value.
Section 1 — Scope and Exclusions

This Article governs subscriptions, memberships, automatic renewals, digital platforms, software and cloud services, streaming, telecommunications, internet access, gaming, data storage, AI services, monitoring, maintenance plans, repair plans, support programs, delivery memberships, fitness and recreation memberships, education services, transportation memberships, connected-vehicle services, recurring deliveries, and similar continuing or access-based consumer arrangements. It does not govern rent, mortgages, lending, credit agreements, insurance, taxes, government fees, court-ordered payments, or ordinary installment purchases resulting in ownership without a continuing service.
Section 2 — Consumer Service Record and Dashboard

Every covered service shall maintain a standardized record stating provider, service, price, billing frequency, enrollment, renewal, benefits, limitations, advertising, data practices, cancellation, refunds, change history, and consent history. An interoperable national dashboard shall allow consumers to view active services, monthly and annualized cost, billing and renewal dates, trials, changes, and cancellation methods without permitting government sale or commercial profiling of preferences.
Section 3 — Price Continuity and Non-Regression

Once a consumer enters at an agreed recurring price, the provider may not increase that price while providing a materially equivalent service. Once at least one person subscribes to a defined tier, the tier may not regress for existing subscribers. Prohibited regression includes adding advertisements to an ad-free service, increasing ad intrusion, removing features, reducing quality, storage, usage, performance, devices, interoperability, privacy, support, or access, converting included benefits into paid add-ons, or manufacturing restrictions to force an upgrade.
Section 4 — Genuine Higher Tiers
A higher-priced plan must add substantial products, services, content categories, functionality, productive capacity, usage, physical service, or professional capability.

Restoring removed features, removing newly added ads, ordinary bug or security fixes, ordinary quality, customer support necessary for use, renaming, repackaging, and artificial separation of integrated features do not constitute added value.
Section 5 — Renewal, Trials, Cancellation, and Dormant Services

Automatic renewal requires affirmative enrollment and clear notice before substantial, annual, discounted-to-full-price, or free-to-paid renewal. Cancellation shall be no harder than enrollment, available through the same medium, immediate in effect for future billing, and free of retention interviews, hidden pages, unnecessary calls, certified mail, delay, or explanation requirements. Dormant services shall trigger a notice showing inactivity, amount paid, next charge, and direct cancellation; prolonged unused service may require renewed consent.
Section 6 — Bundles, Embedded Features, and Dependency

A provider may not force unrelated bundles, remove individually available service to compel a bundle, hide component prices, or move existing benefits into a higher-cost package. A physical capability already included in a purchased product may not require recurring payment unless its operation creates a genuine ongoing provider cost clearly disclosed before purchase. Purchased physical products shall remain reasonably functional without unrelated cloud services.
Section 7 — Digital Property Purchase

A one-time payment for software, games, applications, downloadable content, films, television, music, audiobooks, electronic books, publications, art, educational materials, tools, upgrades, or other digital goods creates a permanent property interest when a reasonable consumer expects indefinite personal use. Labeling the transaction a license, entitlement, activation, or permission does not defeat its substance.
Section 8 — No Revocation of Digital Licenses

A provider shall not revoke, expire, disable, confiscate, or materially impair lawfully purchased digital property after payment, except for fraud, lawful payment reversal, final judicial determination that possession is unlawful, voluntary transfer, or a court remedy directly related to misuse of that property. Unrelated account violations shall not confiscate separate purchases, and account suspension shall not eliminate access to owned digital property.
Section 9 — Offline Access and Local Operation

Digital property shall support offline installation, local authentication, local storage, backup, restoration, and continued use during network or provider failure wherever technically practical. Continuous internet connection may not be required solely for advertising, surveillance, behavioral collection, resale prevention, or commercial control.

Products inherently dependent on live interaction, remote computation, streaming, social networking, marketplaces, or continuously updated data may remain online-dependent if disclosed clearly before payment.
Section 10 — Online Service Continuity Plan

Any online-dependent product accepting consumer payments shall maintain a public continuity plan describing expected duration, infrastructure dependencies, planned and unexpected shutdown, notice, data export, refund, credit, preservation, and transfer. A provider shall not accept substantial payment while knowingly planning imminent termination without prominent disclosure.
Section 11 — Planned and Unexpected Shutdown

Planned permanent shutdown shall ordinarily provide twelve months notice and pursue offline conversion, release of server software, community servers, transfer to another provider, interoperability, removal of authentication, technical documentation, preservation of purchases, refunds, or credits. Unexpected shutdown due to bankruptcy, acquisition, infrastructure failure, supplier loss, dissolution, or government action gives consumers a priority claim for unused or destroyed value against the provider, successor, acquirer, or bankruptcy estate.
Section 12 — Compensation for Lost Online Value

Prepaid and fixed-term service shall be refunded for the unused period. Recently purchased online-dependent products and persistent digital purchases shall receive compensation based on price, time since purchase, expected useful duration, actual availability, remaining value, promised permanence, and ability to transfer. Compensation may be money, proportional refund, equal or greater credit, successor access, or permanent offline access, but credit shall not require substantial additional spending.
Section 13 — Shutdown Reserves and Successor Liability

Large providers may be required to maintain reserves, escrow, continuity trusts, or equivalent security proportionate to consumers, prepaid value, persistent purchases, financial stability, and shutdown cost. Acquirers, successor entities, asset buyers, and reorganized companies acquire continuity and non-regression obligations. A merger or renaming cannot erase consumer rights.
Section 14 — Preservation, Repair, Transfer, and Inheritance

Abandoned products should be preserved through removal of authentication, release of executables, server software, source code where safe, archival transfer, technical documentation, offline modes, community operation, or a National Digital Preservation Repository. Owners may develop compatibility tools, replacement servers, emulators, and repair software for continued lawful use. Digital property shall be transferable, giftable, inheritable, and resalable where technically practical, with anti-duplication controls allowed.
Section 15 — Algorithmic Manipulation and Enforcement

Providers shall not use behavioral data or AI to create cancellation obstacles, conceal lower-cost plans, selectively increase price based on dependency, or exploit psychological vulnerability. Remedies include restoration, baseline enforcement, refunds, credits, compensation, statutory damages, mandatory offline functionality, preservation release, executive clawbacks, monitoring, probation, blacklisting, and charter revocation for persistent abuse.
