This Article governs subscriptions, memberships, automatic renewals, digital platforms, software and cloud services, streaming, telecommunications, internet access, gaming, data storage, AI services, monitoring, maintenance plans, repair plans, support programs, delivery memberships, fitness and recreation memberships, education services, transportation memberships, connected-vehicle services, recurring deliveries, and similar continuing or access-based consumer arrangements. It does not govern rent, mortgages, lending, credit agreements, insurance, taxes, government fees, court-ordered payments, or ordinary installment purchases resulting in ownership without a continuing service.
Section 2 — Consumer Service Record and Dashboard

Every covered service shall maintain a standardized record stating provider, service, price, billing frequency, enrollment, renewal, benefits, limitations, advertising, data practices, cancellation, refunds, change history, and consent history. An interoperable national dashboard shall allow consumers to view active services, monthly and annualized cost, billing and renewal dates, trials, changes, and cancellation methods without permitting government sale or commercial profiling of preferences.
Section 3 — Price Continuity and Non-Regression

Once a consumer enters at an agreed recurring price, the provider may not increase that price while providing a materially equivalent service. Once at least one person subscribes to a defined tier, the tier may not regress for existing subscribers. Prohibited regression includes adding advertisements to an ad-free service, increasing ad intrusion, removing features, reducing quality, storage, usage, performance, devices, interoperability, privacy, support, or access, converting included benefits into paid add-ons, or manufacturing restrictions to force an upgrade.
Section 4 — Genuine Higher Tiers
A higher-priced plan must add substantial products, services, content categories, functionality, productive capacity, usage, physical service, or professional capability.

Restoring removed features, removing newly added ads, ordinary bug or security fixes, ordinary quality, customer support necessary for use, renaming, repackaging, and artificial separation of integrated features do not constitute added value.
Section 5 — Renewal, Trials, Cancellation, and Dormant Services

Automatic renewal requires affirmative enrollment and clear notice before substantial, annual, discounted-to-full-price, or free-to-paid renewal. Cancellation shall be no harder than enrollment, available through the same medium, immediate in effect for future billing, and free of retention interviews, hidden pages, unnecessary calls, certified mail, delay, or explanation requirements. Dormant services shall trigger a notice showing inactivity, amount paid, next charge, and direct cancellation; prolonged unused service may require renewed consent.
Section 6 — Bundles, Embedded Features, and Dependency

A provider may not force unrelated bundles, remove individually available service to compel a bundle, hide component prices, or move existing benefits into a higher-cost package. A physical capability already included in a purchased product may not require recurring payment unless its operation creates a genuine ongoing provider cost clearly disclosed before purchase. Purchased physical products shall remain reasonably functional without unrelated cloud services.
Section 7 — Digital Property Purchase

A one-time payment for software, games, applications, downloadable content, films, television, music, audiobooks, electronic books, publications, art, educational materials, tools, upgrades, or other digital goods creates a permanent property interest when a reasonable consumer expects indefinite personal use. Labeling the transaction a license, entitlement, activation, or permission does not defeat its substance.
Section 8 — No Revocation of Digital Licenses

A provider shall not revoke, expire, disable, confiscate, or materially impair lawfully purchased digital property after payment, except for fraud, lawful payment reversal, final judicial determination that possession is unlawful, voluntary transfer, or a court remedy directly related to misuse of that property. Unrelated account violations shall not confiscate separate purchases, and account suspension shall not eliminate access to owned digital property.
Section 9 — Offline Access and Local Operation

Digital property shall support offline installation, local authentication, local storage, backup, restoration, and continued use during network or provider failure wherever technically practical. Continuous internet connection may not be required solely for advertising, surveillance, behavioral collection, resale prevention, or commercial control.

Products inherently dependent on live interaction, remote computation, streaming, social networking, marketplaces, or continuously updated data may remain online-dependent if disclosed clearly before payment.
Section 10 — Online Service Continuity Plan

Any online-dependent product accepting consumer payments shall maintain a public continuity plan describing expected duration, infrastructure dependencies, planned and unexpected shutdown, notice, data export, refund, credit, preservation, and transfer. A provider shall not accept substantial payment while knowingly planning imminent termination without prominent disclosure.
Section 11 — Planned and Unexpected Shutdown

Planned permanent shutdown shall ordinarily provide twelve months notice and pursue offline conversion, release of server software, community servers, transfer to another provider, interoperability, removal of authentication, technical documentation, preservation of purchases, refunds, or credits. Unexpected shutdown due to bankruptcy, acquisition, infrastructure failure, supplier loss, dissolution, or government action gives consumers a priority claim for unused or destroyed value against the provider, successor, acquirer, or bankruptcy estate.
Section 12 — Compensation for Lost Online Value

Prepaid and fixed-term service shall be refunded for the unused period. Recently purchased online-dependent products and persistent digital purchases shall receive compensation based on price, time since purchase, expected useful duration, actual availability, remaining value, promised permanence, and ability to transfer. Compensation may be money, proportional refund, equal or greater credit, successor access, or permanent offline access, but credit shall not require substantial additional spending.
Section 13 — Shutdown Reserves and Successor Liability

Large providers may be required to maintain reserves, escrow, continuity trusts, or equivalent security proportionate to consumers, prepaid value, persistent purchases, financial stability, and shutdown cost. Acquirers, successor entities, asset buyers, and reorganized companies acquire continuity and non-regression obligations. A merger or renaming cannot erase consumer rights.
Section 14 — Preservation, Repair, Transfer, and Inheritance

Abandoned products should be preserved through removal of authentication, release of executables, server software, source code where safe, archival transfer, technical documentation, offline modes, community operation, or a National Digital Preservation Repository. Owners may develop compatibility tools, replacement servers, emulators, and repair software for continued lawful use. Digital property shall be transferable, giftable, inheritable, and resalable where technically practical, with anti-duplication controls allowed.
Section 15 — Algorithmic Manipulation and Enforcement

Providers shall not use behavioral data or AI to create cancellation obstacles, conceal lower-cost plans, selectively increase price based on dependency, or exploit psychological vulnerability. Remedies include restoration, baseline enforcement, refunds, credits, compensation, statutory damages, mandatory offline functionality, preservation release, executive clawbacks, monitoring, probation, blacklisting, and charter revocation for persistent abuse.

articles.title: National Identity, Flag, and the American Builder Beaver
articles.summary: The constitutional order shall possess national symbols reflecting construction, continuity, measurement, productive labor, resource stewardship, and resilience rather than conquest or domination.
sections: Section 2 — Meaning of the American Builder Beaver
excerpt: The constitutional order shall possess national symbols reflecting construction, continuity, measurement, productive labor, resource stewardship, and resilience rather than conquest or domination.
Section 1 — National Animal

The American Beaver, Castor canadensis, is designated the national animal of the Commonwealth and may be formally styled The American Builder Beaver in constitutional, ceremonial, educational, and governmental use.
Section 2 — Meaning of the American Builder Beaver

The American Beaver represents the values of this constitutional system because it builds functional infrastructure rather than symbolizing conquest; plans, maintains, repairs, expands, and adapts its works over time; manages water, wetlands, habitat, and surrounding resources; creates distributed structures that collectively reshape a region; embodies productive labor aligned with education, manufacturing, repairability, infrastructure, and technological development; rebuilds after damage; belongs naturally and historically to North America; and provides a non-predatory emblem for a government centered on construction, efficiency, measurement, resource circulation, continuity, and adaptation.
Section 3 — Use of the Beaver

textBlocks.text: The constitutional order shall possess national symbols reflecting construction, continuity, measurement, productive labor, resource stewardship, and resilience rather than conquest or domination.
Section 1 — National Animal

The American Beaver, Castor canadensis, is designated the national animal of the Commonwealth and may be formally styled The American Builder Beaver in constitutional, ceremonial, educational, and governmental use.
Section 2 — Meaning of the American Builder Beaver

The American Beaver represents the values of this constitutional system because it builds functional infrastructure rather than symbolizing conquest; plans, maintains, repairs, expands, and adapts its works over time; manages water, wetlands, habitat, and surrounding resources; creates distributed structures that collectively reshape a region; embodies productive labor aligned with education, manufacturing, repairability, infrastructure, and technological development; rebuilds after damage; belongs naturally and historically to North America; and provides a non-predatory emblem for a government centered on construction, efficiency, measurement, resource circulation, continuity, and adaptation.
Section 3 — Use of the Beaver

The beaver shall not appear on the national flag. It may appear on the national seal, currency, official documents, medals, public works insignia, infrastructure agencies, educational material, and other governmental emblems. Any official depiction shall preserve the animal’s constructive and ecological meaning and shall avoid militarized or predatory representation.
The National Flag
Official design representation - proportion 2:3
Section 4 — Flag Design Specification

The national flag shall have a 2:3 proportion and a deep midnight-blue field representing stability, knowledge, and continuity. Five equal vertical pillars shall stand across the center, representing the five age cohorts. The pillars shall be identical in width and height because no cohort possesses constitutional supremacy. Behind the pillars shall be a thin silver circular ring representing the continuous transition of citizens through the cohorts, institutional continuity, the circular economy, resource recovery, and generational succession. At the center shall appear a simplified white eight-point compass star representing evidence, scientific measurement, national direction, and the duty to evaluate policy through measurable outcomes. Along the bottom shall appear a narrow earth-red foundation band representing labor, infrastructure, material production, and the physical foundation upon which the constitutional system operates.
Section 5 — Flag Reproduction

The flag shall remain geometrically simple enough to reproduce from memory. Government shall publish exact dimensional ratios and approved color specifications, but minor manufacturing variation shall not invalidate respectful reproduction. No text, seal, fringe, emblem, commercial mark, or beaver image shall be added to the national flag itself.

institutions.name: Five-Cohort Executive Council
institutions.description: Collective national executive authority, one elected leader per age cohort.

institutions.name: Assembly of State Delegations
institutions.description: Legislative authority organized by state delegations and age-banded representation.

institutions.name: Judiciary and Constitutional Balance
institutions.description: The constitutional judicial branch, including local, appellate, national, and supreme constitutional courts.

institutions.name: State and Local Courts
institutions.description: Trial and local judicial forums within the distributed court structure.

institutions.name: Regional Appellate Courts
institutions.description: Intermediate appellate review across regional jurisdictions.

institutions.name: National High Court
institutions.description: National judicial review beneath the Constitutional Supreme Court.

institutions.name: Constitutional Supreme Court
institutions.description: Final constitutional review and structural balance for the Commonwealth.

institutions.name: National Court for Grave Crimes
institutions.description: Specialized trial forum for nationally grave offenses with full due process and automatic appeal.

institutions.name: States and Municipalities
institutions.description: Distributed administration and retained local authority under the constitutional order.

agencies.name: Office of National Performance and Accountability
agencies.description: ONPA independently measures effectiveness, efficiency, fiscal performance, delivery, transparency, and intergenerational effects.
agencies.articleTitle: The Office of National Performance and Accountability

agencies.name: National Revenue Service
agencies.description: A simplified national revenue institution for individual taxation, public finance, and protected social-support collection.
agencies.articleTitle: The National Revenue Service and Public Finance

agencies.name: Economic Registry
agencies.description: A state and municipal registry for aggregate local productive capacity, employment, commerce, and economic conditions.
agencies.articleTitle: The Local Productive Economy, Employment Registry, and Consumer Commerce

agencies.name: Employment Registry
agencies.description: The employment-facing registry for productive work opportunities, local hiring, and labor-market coordination.
agencies.articleTitle: The Local Productive Economy, Employment Registry, and Consumer Commerce

agencies.name: National Talent Service
agencies.description: A public talent service for linking skills, training, and useful work within the local productive economy.
agencies.articleTitle: The Local Productive Economy, Employment Registry, and Consumer Commerce

agencies.name: National Agency for Technological Advancement and Human Safety
agencies.description: The agency charged with accelerating technological advancement while protecting human safety and child development.
agencies.articleTitle: Technological Advancement and Human Safety

agencies.name: Office of Human Impact
agencies.description: An independent office investigating injury, disease, displacement, dependency, manipulation, or child-development harm from technology.
agencies.articleTitle: Technological Advancement and Human Safety

agencies.name: Central Materials Recovery and Recycling Authority
agencies.description: The authority establishing material recovery standards, producer responsibility, reuse, recycling, and landfill elimination.
agencies.articleTitle: Material Circulation, Resource Recovery, and the Elimination of Landfills

agencies.name: National Continuity and Infrastructure Stability Authority
agencies.description: The authority maintaining minimum infrastructure standards, continuity cells, reserves, degraded-operation plans, and restoration readiness.
agencies.articleTitle: Homeland Infrastructure Stability and Civilization Continuity

agencies.name: Regional Survival Commands
agencies.description: Regional operational commands within Survival Mode for continuity, restoration, and emergency distribution.
agencies.articleTitle: National Survival Mode and the Preservation of Civil Society

agencies.name: Geographic Continuity Cells
agencies.description: Local continuity units for degraded operations, restoration sequencing, and civil-society preservation.
agencies.articleTitle: National Survival Mode and the Preservation of Civil Society

agencies.name: National Homelessness Elimination Program
agencies.description: A national program to eliminate homelessness through shelter, food, water, sanitation, medical care, and safe placement.
agencies.articleTitle: Domestic Human Security, Homelessness Elimination, and Suspension of Foreign Aid

agencies.name: National Safe Haven Program
agencies.description: A secure, clean, non-carceral housing and stabilization program for people requiring protective placement.
agencies.articleTitle: Domestic Human Security, Homelessness Elimination, and Suspension of Foreign Aid

agencies.name: National Housing Authority
agencies.description: The authority for ownership limits, lawful prices, nonprofit rent, first ownership, and commercial conversion into housing.
agencies.articleTitle: Housing Access, Limited Ownership, Nonprofit Transfer, and Income-Based Occupancy

agencies.name: National Residential Exchange
agencies.description: A housing exchange for lawful transfers, nonprofit conversion, and anti-speculation residential movement.
agencies.articleTitle: Housing Access, Limited Ownership, Nonprofit Transfer, and Income-Based Occupancy

agencies.name: National Rail Development and Mobility Authority
agencies.description: The independent authority planning corridors, state capital hubs, national rail lines, mobility access, and interoperability.
agencies.articleTitle: National Rail Revitalization, Mobility Access, and Transportation Independence

agencies.name: Rail Technology Program
agencies.description: A technology program for rail design, manufacturing, repair, control systems, safety, and continuity.
agencies.articleTitle: National Rail Revitalization, Mobility Access, and Transportation Independence

agencies.name: Retired Veteran School Protection Corps
agencies.description: A school protection corps using qualified retired veterans under training, oversight, and civilian educational authority.
agencies.articleTitle: National Education, Child Development, Productive Learning, and School Security

agencies.name: National Health Intelligence Network
agencies.description: A de-identified medical data network with state nodes, national analysis, independent backups, and research protections.
agencies.articleTitle: National Health Intelligence, De-Identified Medical Data, and Preventive Care

agencies.name: National Health Data Council
agencies.description: An oversight council including clinicians, mental-health professionals, patient advocates, data experts, and public representatives.
agencies.articleTitle: National Health Intelligence, De-Identified Medical Data, and Preventive Care

agencies.name: National Circular Sanitation Authority
agencies.description: The authority leading the transition from water-carried sewage to circular sanitation, water conservation, and resource recovery.
agencies.articleTitle: National Circular Sanitation, Water Conservation, and Elimination of Conventional Water-Carried Sewage
